screening_results.reason: Abstract explicitly describes this paper as a review of neuroimaging and neuromodulation approaches rather than reporting original task-based fMRI experiments. It does not report task-based problem-solving fMRI results in healthy adults. Excluded under E1 (review/meta-analysis).

Abstract reads as a conceptual/review-style paper on neurophysiology of functional imaging and discusses sensory stimulation and awareness, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-based problem-solving or reasoning results. Therefore exclude as a review/article rather than a primary task-based problem-solving fMRI study.

This article is a review summarizing fMRI, PET, and connectivity studies in anesthesia and analgesia rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

The manuscript is a methodological primer/review of fMRI techniques rather than an original task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in humans. It fits exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis), so it should be excluded.

This paper is a review/meta-analysis of fMRI studies in schizophrenia rather than a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It focuses on patient vs control comparisons and does not report primary data on problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This is a review/overview article summarizing methods and applications of real-time fMRI rather than an original, task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; therefore it meets the review exclusion criterion.

The article is explicitly a review of neuroimaging findings on caffeine and cognition, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis). No primary task-based problem-solving fMRI results reported for healthy adults.

Abstract explicitly states this is a review of PET and MR activation studies (a secondary review), not an original task-based fMRI experiment. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses (E1).

This is a review article discussing fMRI BOLD signal relationships rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activation results for a problem-solving task.

This article is a review/discussion comparing BOLD and electrophysiological signals in V1 rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-based problem-solving activation; thus it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

This paper is a review and meta-analysis (not an original task-based fMRI experiment). It does not report a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults and therefore meets exclusion criterion E1.
The abstract explicitly states this is a review describing fMRI methodology rather than reporting original task-based fMRI experiments in healthy adults, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

This is a review article of functional neuroimaging in pediatric medulloblastoma patients, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).
The study reports simultaneous intracortical recordings and fMRI in monkeys and is not a task-based fMRI study of human problem solving. No healthy adult human participants are included.
Pre-clinical PET study in conscious monkeys (non-human) using PET and microdialysis, not task-based fMRI in humans. Does not meet human fMRI problem-solving criteria.

This is a methods/review article discussing PET, SPECT, and fMRI and the effects of aging and drugs, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review/meta-analytic-type paper, so exclusion criterion E1 applies.

This is a review paper summarizing fMRI studies of somatosensory perception (touch, pain), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses and does not address problem-solving tasks.

This paper is explicitly described as a review of fMRI studies rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses (E1).

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI BOLD response to a challenging cognitive task (so I1 and I4 satisfied). It does not specify whether the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task or explicitly that healthy adults (18–65) were included, so full-text review is needed to confirm I2 and I3.

Abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study of symbolic algebra transformations and reports task-related BOLD activation changes (left prefrontal, parietal, motor). It does not specify participant ages/health, so inclusion of healthy adults (I3) cannot be confirmed from the abstract; include for full-text review to verify.

This is a task-based fMRI study of speech comprehension, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning). It does meet I1 and I4 but is not relevant to the review objective.

This is a review article synthesizing BOLD-fMRI studies of migraine pathophysiology rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).
Study used PET (not fMRI) and investigated patients with post-stroke Wernicke's aphasia rather than healthy adults; does not meet fMRI or healthy adult criteria.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study where healthy participants modulate single-trial BOLD signals to answer multiple-choice questions, and presents task-related activation/decoding results. However it does not clearly state that the tasks involve problem-solving/reasoning nor specify participant ages (18–65). Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a PET study (non-fMRI) of odor imagery rather than a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI). Although participants are healthy adults, the imaging modality and task do not match the review objective.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of self-agency during voluntary finger movements, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does include healthy adults and reports task activations, but does not address the review objective (problem solving).

The abstract states this is a review chapter summarizing fMRI findings rather than reporting original task-based fMRI experiments in healthy adults. It does not present new task-related activation results. Excluded as a review (E1).

This is a task-based fMRI study of analogizing/reasoning tasks that reports task-related neural activations. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or health status, so inclusion for full-text review is appropriate to confirm healthy adult sample.

This is a review article summarizing functional neuroimaging studies of vagus nerve stimulation, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

The task is picture naming (naming actions/objects), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. The study does include task-based fMRI and reports activations but does not satisfy inclusion criterion I2.
Abstract describes a review of fMRI feedback methodology rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; it is a review article. Exclusion criterion E1 applies.

Abstract reports multimodal imaging during a working memory task in women with PMDD; it does not indicate inclusion of a healthy adult sample (18-65). Review objective requires healthy adults performing problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Therefore exclusion criterion E3 applies.

This is a PET study of script-driven traumatic imagery (regional cerebral blood flow), not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving. Participants are trauma-exposed individuals with/without PTSD rather than a clear healthy adult problem-solving sample. Primary clear exclusion: non-fMRI modality.

The article is an introductory/review piece on simultaneous EEG-fMRI methods rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, meeting the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

This is a PET study (H2(15)O rCBF), not task-based fMRI, so it fails the modality inclusion criterion (I1). Additionally, the task is auditory attention rather than problem-solving. Exclusion justified based on non-fMRI methodology.

The study is a task-based fMRI paper that reports task-related activations and effective connectivity, but the tasks are haptic/imagery/perception rather than explicit problem-solving or reasoning. The abstract does not clearly state it involves problem-solving, so include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.
The study uses PET (rCBF with oxygen-15 and carbon-10 tracers), not fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement. Therefore it must be excluded (non-fMRI study).

Abstract describes task-based fMRI mental imagery in patients with severe brain injury; no healthy adult (18–65) participants are mentioned and the study focuses on patient responses. Exclude for lack of healthy adult sample.

The study uses task-based fMRI in patients with disorders of consciousness (mental imagery) to predict recovery; it does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample and the tasks are not problem-solving/reasoning in healthy adults. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample).

This is an observational study of cerebral blood flow related to hemoglobin in older adults; the abstract does not describe a task-based fMRI procedure or any problem-solving/reasoning task. It is not a task-based fMRI study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Task-based fMRI with motor and cognitive tasks and reports task-related activation/BOLD measures; includes a healthy young adult group (ages 19-27). The abstract does not specify that the cognitive task is a problem-solving or reasoning task, so inclusion based on I2 is unclear — include for full-text review to confirm.

Abstract reports that healthy, normal (right-handed) subjects performed task-based fMRI (semantic and numerical judgment, sentence comprehension). However, tasks appear language/semantic rather than explicit problem-solving; the abstract lacks clear evidence that tasks are problem-solving/reasoning or detailed task-activation results. Insufficient detail to confirm I2/I4, so include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study of verbal fluency that includes children and adults, implying inclusion of healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations. Abstract does not make clear whether the verbal fluency task meets the review's ‘problem-solving or reasoning’ definition, so include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study using the Tower of Hanoi problem-solving task with human participants, reports BOLD activation patterns in specific brain regions, and links results to behavior—meeting I1–I4.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI reproducibility study but does not specify that the task involved problem solving/reasoning or that participants were healthy adults. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3; include for full-text review.

The study uses task-based fMRI with healthy control participants and reports activation, but the task is a semantic language (semantic decision/tone decision) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). Therefore it does not address the review objective.

The study used PET ([(15)O] water), not fMRI, and investigated visually induced sadness rather than problem-solving tasks. Participants were healthy elderly (likely >65), so not the target 18–65 adult sample. Exclude based on non-fMRI modality.

This is a task-based fMRI study of deductive reasoning in 12 healthy adult participants, reporting BOLD activation patterns during relational and conditional problem-solving tasks. Fits all inclusion criteria and does not meet any exclusion criteria.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), the task involves perception and recognition of facial emotional expressions (social-emotion cognition), not problem-solving or reasoning. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 and is excluded.

This is a review of resting-state/spontaneous brain activity and altered states of consciousness (including PET and resting-state fMRI), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activations in healthy adult participants.

This is a task-based fMRI study in a pediatric bipolar disorder clinical sample investigating pharmacotherapy effects; the abstract does not report any healthy adult (18–65) participants or a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it fails the required healthy adult sample criterion.

Abstract describes a task-based BOLD fMRI study reporting task-related activation in healthy subjects and schizophrenics (working memory tasks). However it does not clearly state the tasks involved are problem-solving or reasoning. Insufficient information to determine I2, so include for full-text review.

This is a PET (positron emission tomography) study of combat-related stimuli in veterans with and without PTSD, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem-solving tasks.

This study used [15O] water PET (not fMRI), so it is a non-fMRI imaging study (E4). Additionally, the task was self-paced counting/tapping rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task, but the primary exclusion is non-fMRI.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study combining rs-fMRI with PET/MRS to investigate the DMN during rest; no task-based problem-solving or reasoning task is reported. Thus it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

Study used PET in conscious rhesus monkeys (non-human) and did not involve task-based fMRI in healthy adult humans; also did not investigate problem-solving tasks. Excluded based on E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E4 (non-fMRI study).

The fMRI task involves internal/external awareness (heartbeat and tone counting), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. The study otherwise uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation, but does not address the review objective.

This is a PET study (O-15 H2O) rather than task-based fMRI, and the task (eyeblink associative conditioning) is not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Although it includes healthy adult controls and reports task-related brain activation, it fails the fMRI requirement.

The study used PET (positron emission tomography), not task-based fMRI, so it meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study). Although it reports task-related brain activation during a working-memory task, the imaging modality is not fMRI and the task is working memory (not problem-solving), so it does not meet the review's I1/I2 requirements.
Not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It uses PET to measure resting/sleep brain activity related to dream recall, so it is a non-fMRI study and a resting-state/sleep study.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants with reported task-related BOLD activation. Abstract does not specify that tasks are problem-solving or reasoning, so include for full-text review to determine relevance.

The study used PET (not fMRI) and investigates eye gaze/theory-of-mind processing rather than problem-solving. It therefore does not meet the requirement for task-based fMRI studies of problem solving and is excluded based on non-fMRI modality.

This study used PET (regional cerebral blood flow) rather than task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement. Additionally, the task was working-memory (not clearly problem-solving) and participants were aged 50–70 (outside the specified 18–65 target), reinforcing exclusion.

Abstract studies cerebral blood flow in Alzheimer patients, not a task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. No healthy adult (18-65) sample is reported and modality is not fMRI, so it fails inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI BOLD responses during emotional and cognitive processing in unipolar depression, but does not specify problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor clearly state a healthy adult group. Insufficient information to determine eligibility — include for full-text review.
Abstract explicitly states this article is a review. The meta-analysis requires primary task-based fMRI studies in healthy adults; as a review article it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This is a task-based fMRI study of visual perception (continuous flash suppression detecting visible vs invisible tools), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Excluded because the study used [15O]water PET rather than task-based fMRI required by the review. Additionally, the tasks are memory/working memory rather than explicit problem-solving, so it does not match the fMRI problem-solving focus.

Task is a gender-discrimination (face perception) fMRI study, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activation, it does not meet the review’s requirement for a problem-solving task (I2 not satisfied).
Task-based BOLD fMRI study of verbal-reasoning and spatial problem-solving tasks in healthy adult participants, reporting task-related brain activation results; satisfies all inclusion criteria.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study linking resting glutamate to BOLD responses during a cognitive-control task and reports task-related activation; however, it does not specify that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task or provide participant age/health details. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow, not an fMRI study, and it investigates memory tasks (recognition), not problem-solving or reasoning. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis modality or task requirements.

Abstract reports task-evoked BOLD responses (task-based fMRI) and provides task-related activation results, but does not specify that the cognitive task was a problem-solving/reasoning task nor give participant ages to confirm an 18–65 healthy adult group. Include for full-text review to assess I2 and I3.

Abstract focuses on Theory of Mind/mentalization and does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task, participant age/sample, or explicit task-based fMRI methods. Insufficient information to determine if it meets the review's problem-solving fMRI criteria—include for full-text review.

This is a task-based fMRI study of sentence comprehension (semantic/syntactic processing) in healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4). However, the task is language comprehension rather than problem-solving or reasoning, so it does not meet I2.

The study used Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS), not fMRI, therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement. The task examines vigilance/attention rather than problem solving; primary exclusion is non-fMRI (E4).

The study used task-based fMRI in rabbits performing eyeblink conditioning (an animal model), not human adults or a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it lacks a healthy adult human sample and does not address problem solving (E3).

The study used oxygen-15 PET (regional cerebral blood flow), not task-based fMRI, which violates exclusion criterion E4. Also, the tasks are verbal fluency rather than problem-solving, so it does not address the review objective.
This is a positron emission tomography (PET) study of sentence comprehension/word repetition, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, so it fails the fMRI requirement.

BOLD fMRI study reporting task-related activations during quantifier comprehension, so it meets I1 and I4. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state a healthy adult sample, nor clearly frame the task as problem-solving/reasoning (I2,I3). Include for full-text review to confirm those criteria.

The study uses a cued motor task (motor planning/execution), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. Although it is a task-based fMRI study with adult participants and reports activation, it does not address the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study with 16 healthy adult volunteers performing a number-comparison task and reporting task-related BOLD activations. It is unclear from the abstract whether this task meets the review's definition of ‘problem solving/reasoning’, so include for full-text review.

This is a PET (PiB) study estimating cerebral blood flow, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. Participants are older adults/MCI (elderly controls), not healthy adults aged 18–65. Fails inclusion criteria I1–I3; meets exclusion criteria E3 and E4.

This is a task-based fMRI study of narrative/contextual alignment (listening to vignettes), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails to meet inclusion criterion I2 despite meeting other imaging and sample criteria.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study examining propofol-induced loss of consciousness, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving or reasoning; no task-related activations are reported.

This is an animal (awake rat) study using optical and electrophysiological measures, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults performing problem-solving; therefore it lacks a healthy adult human sample and is a non-fMRI study.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study in healthy young adults reporting task-related BOLD responses (motor/isometric handgrip), it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (does not address the review objective).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of fluid reasoning that reports task-related brain activation. Participant age/health not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study that includes healthy adult controls and reports task-related activation (I1, I3, I4), the task is an auditory cognitive control task rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (does not meet I2).

Event-related task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation during a plausibility judgment (sentence comprehension). The abstract does not specify participant ages and it is unclear whether the task qualifies as problem-solving/reasoning; include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This paper is a systematic review (title/abstract indicate a review), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, so it meets exclusion criterion E1. The abstract does not report primary task-based fMRI results in healthy adult problem-solving participants.

The study targets Alzheimer's disease patients and longitudinal change in fMRI related to neuropsychological performance; the abstract does not report inclusion of healthy adult (18–65) participants or a problem-solving task. It therefore fails I3 (no healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study where participants solved simplified Sudoku (a problem-solving task) and reports BOLD/ROI activation. Age range not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

Study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI inclusion criterion and should be excluded (E4). Although it studies mental-rotation in healthy adults and reports task-related activation, the imaging modality disqualifies it.

Excluded because the study used positron-emission tomography (H2[15]O PET), not fMRI, so it does not meet the requirement for task-based fMRI (E4). Although it involves a problem-solving task (WCST) in healthy adults and reports task-related activation, the imaging modality excludes it from this review.

The study used PET imaging ([(15)O] H2O PET), not fMRI; thus it fails the fMRI requirement (E4). Although it involves healthy adult participants performing reasoning tasks and reports activation (I2, I3, I4), the imaging modality excludes it from this review.

This is a methodological/review paper discussing neurostatistical approaches across methods (single-cell, fMRI, MEG) rather than reporting a task-based fMRI experiment of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not present primary task-related fMRI activation results for a healthy adult problem-solving task.

Study reports simultaneous intracortical recordings and BOLD fMRI in awake monkeys (non-human). It does not involve human participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails the requirement for a healthy adult human sample and task relevance.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI with feedback/self-regulation in 12 healthy participants and reports task-related BOLD activations. However, the task is BCI neurofeedback/self-regulation rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task; age range not provided. Include for full-text review to verify whether the task fits the review's definition of problem solving and confirm participant ages.

This study reports resting-state functional connectivity in anaesthetized (non-human) monkeys, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans. It is therefore excluded as a resting-state/connectivity study and because it does not include a healthy adult human sample.

The study used H2(15)O PET (non-fMRI) rather than task-based fMRI, and the tasks were visual attention and motor learning rather than problem-solving. Although healthy controls were included, the imaging modality and task domain do not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This paper is a systematic review of molecular imaging studies (not an original task-based fMRI study). It therefore meets exclusion criteria E1 (review/meta-analysis) and E4 (non-fMRI molecular imaging).

The study uses finger tapping and reaction-time tasks (simple and four-choice RT), which are motor/RT tasks rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by I2. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports activation, but does not meet the review’s requirement for problem-solving/reasoning tasks.

Study maps regional cerebral blood flow during clonidine infusion in volunteers with no task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm reported and does not specify fMRI imaging—therefore non-fMRI/non-task and not relevant.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation (CASL p-fMRI and BOLD) reporting task-related brain activation and CBF quantification during a color-word Stroop cognitive task. However, the abstract does not specify participant ages or health status, and the Stroop task may not clearly fit the review's ‘problem-solving’ definition. Include for full-text review to verify whether the task meets the problem-solving criterion and whether the sample includes healthy adults (18-65).

The study uses resting-state functional connectivity (fc-BOLD) and structural measures in older adults and aMCI, not a task-based fMRI problem-solving/reasoning paradigm. This meets the exclusion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, and investigated respiratory discomfort during loaded breathing rather than problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the ‘fMRI’ requirement.

Study involves dissociative identity disorder patients undergoing trauma-related symptom provocation, not healthy adult participants, and it does not examine problem-solving tasks. Abstract provides no indication of task-based fMRI in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.
This is a task-based fMRI study of episodic memory encoding rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review objective, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
This study used PET (positron-emission tomography), not fMRI, and examined the hypnotic state rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails the fMRI requirement for this review.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study examining spontaneous BOLD correlations with no task, and does not involve any problem-solving or reasoning task in participants, so it fails inclusion criteria for task-based studies.

Participants are described as "healthy elderly adults," which are outside the review’s target age range (18–65). Although this is a task-based fMRI problem-solving study reporting BOLD responses, the age mismatch means it does not include the required healthy adult group for inclusion.
Non-human primate PET study (Japanese monkeys) — not a human sample and not an fMRI study. Does not meet task-based fMRI in healthy adults.
This is a PET ([15O]H2O) neuroimaging study, not an fMRI study. The review specifically requires task-based fMRI; therefore the study meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI).

This paper is a review of fMRI studies (a review article), not an original task-based fMRI experiment reporting new task-related activation in participants. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

Study examines fMRI responses to drug craving in cocaine-dependent patients (cue-reactivity), not a problem-solving/reasoning task, and does not report a healthy adult (18–65) sample. Thus it fails the healthy-sample requirement.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, and investigated imaginal aggressive behavior rather than problem-solving tasks. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not address the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study that includes the Tower of London, a problem-solving/planning task. Abstract confirms fMRI during motor and cognitive tasks but does not explicitly state healthy adult sample or report specific activation results; include for full-text review to confirm I3 and I4.

Study is a task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports task-related BOLD activation. However, the task is probabilistic reversal learning (cognitive flexibility), which may not clearly meet 'problem solving or reasoning'; include for full-text review to verify task relevance.
Study is of first-episode schizophrenia patients receiving quetiapine; no healthy adult sample is described, so it does not meet the requirement for healthy adults.

This is a PET (15O-water rCBF) study, not an fMRI study; thus it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement and meets exclusion criterion E4. Participants did perform a problem-solving task, but imaging modality excludes it.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of emotional (angry/fearful face) evaluation and amygdala modulation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it meets I1 and I4, it does not satisfy I2 (problem-solving), so it does not address the review objective.

The study examined only stroke survivors (no healthy adult sample) and focused on effective connectivity (DCM) analyses rather than task-related activation maps. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for no healthy adult sample and connectivity-only focus.

This is a review article discussing resting-state and theoretical frameworks for disorders of consciousness, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activation for problem-solving tasks.

This study examines fALFF (low-frequency BOLD fluctuations) related to acupuncture at PC6, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It appears to be a resting-state/fALFF study rather than a task-based cognitive fMRI study, so it does not meet the review focus.
The study only reports task-based fMRI in multiple sclerosis patients and does not include a healthy adult sample (18–65). Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (No healthy adult sample).

Study used PET (not fMRI), so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement. The task involves sensorimotor conflict rather than problem-solving, but the clear exclusion is that it is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study.

This study used resting-state fMRI in infants (first two years) and examined spontaneous BOLD frequency and behavior. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving nor does it include healthy adults, so it fails the review population and task requirements.
The study used PET ([15O] water) rCBF, not task-based fMRI, so it fails the imaging modality requirement for inclusion.

The article is a review focusing on perfusion-based fMRI insights from animal studies, not a primary task-based fMRI study of humans performing problem-solving tasks. It is a review and does not include healthy adult human participants.
This is a PET study measuring resting/regional cerebral blood flow, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement.

This is an animal (rat) study investigating spontaneous hemodynamic/BOLD fluctuations under anesthesia (resting-like, burst-suppression). It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans. Exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only) apply.

Study uses task-based fMRI of mental imagery of posture and gait (not a problem-solving or reasoning task), so it fails inclusion criterion I2 ('problem-solving or reasoning task'). Other inclusion criteria (I1, I3, I4) are met.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports task-related activation, but it does not clearly state that participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task (tasks listed: working memory, spatial attention, word generation). Unclear if these meet the review's definition of problem solving; include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Study is an fMRI study in conscious rhesus monkeys (non-human) using pharmacological and visual stimuli; no human healthy adult participants and not a problem-solving task. Excluded because it does not include a healthy adult human sample.

This is a hypothesis/review-style article about spontaneous (resting-state) cortical patterns, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. It discusses resting-state correlations and theory rather than reporting task-related activations in healthy adults.

The fMRI task is a visuomotor attention task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). The study does include task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports task activations, but it does not address problem solving as required by the review.

The study used a working memory task (fMRI) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task central to the review objective, so it does not address the target task domain. It does report task-related activations and is task-based fMRI, but I2 (problem-solving/reasoning) is not met from the abstract.

This is a task-based fMRI study of emotional facial expression processing in healthy adults reporting task-related BOLD activation, but the task is emotional processing (implicit/explicit), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow during an auditory attention task in recreational marijuana users—not a task-based fMRI study nor a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it fails inclusion I1/I2 and meets exclusion E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a PET ([11C]doxepin) study measuring histamine H1 receptor binding in Alzheimer’s and control subjects, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Does not report task-related fMRI activations. Excluded due to being a non-fMRI study.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in healthy estrogen-treated postmenopausal women but does not specify that the tasks are problem-solving/reasoning nor report ages or explicit task-related activation in the abstract. Insufficient information to determine I2, I3, or I4, so include for full-text review.
The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement for inclusion.

This is a resting-state fMRI study analyzing spontaneous BOLD fluctuations (slow-4/slow-5) with no task or problem-solving/reasoning paradigm reported. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation during a visuospatial task, but it does not clearly state the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor explicitly confirm healthy adult (18-65) participants. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

The study is task-based fMRI using a cognitive Stroop task, but the abstract does not clearly indicate a problem-solving or reasoning task (per review objective) nor explicitly state participant age/health. Because it’s unclear whether the task fits the review’s definition of problem solving and participant details are incomplete, include for full-text review.

Animal PET study in monkeys using [15O]H2O and FDG; not an fMRI study and does not involve human adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet fMRI or human healthy adult criteria.

Participants are adolescents (mean age 16.8), so the study does not include a healthy adult sample (age 18-65). Although it is a task-based fMRI using the Tower of London, the absence of adults clearly excludes it from this review.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activations during a mental rotation (visuospatial/reasoning) task in young and older human participants. Fits inclusion criteria though age ranges are not specified in abstract; include for full-text review to confirm participant ages and task fits the problem-solving definition.

The study investigates patients in a persistent vegetative state and examines metabolism/functional connectivity between brainstem and cortex. It is not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults; there is no healthy adult sample and the focus is connectivity/metabolism rather than task-evoked problem solving.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults with task-related activation reported, but the task (Stroop) is an executive/inhibition task and not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task. Abstract is insufficient to confirm I2, so include for full-text review.

Task-based BOLD fMRI performed in healthy adult participants performing a verbal Trail Making Test; reports task-related activations. It is unclear from the abstract whether the vTMT meets the review's definition of a ‘problem-solving or reasoning’ task, so include for full-text screening.
Study used conscious rats (animal subjects) with intracerebroventricular cocaine challenge—no human participants and therefore no healthy adult sample relevant to the review.

The study uses task-based fMRI during motor movements and motor imagery, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet the review’s required task domain (I2). The abstract does report task-related BOLD activation and uses task-based fMRI (I1, I4).

The study used PET (H2(15)O) rather than task-based fMRI, so it fails the fMRI modality requirement (E4). The tasks reported are spatial working memory, not problem-solving, reinforcing that it does not meet the review objective. Excluding on modality is clear from the abstract.

The study uses task-based fMRI of motor imagery (brisk walking), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. It likely reports task activations and includes humans, but the task domain does not match the review objective.

The abstract reports a task-based fMRI study that applied IVA to real task paradigms and reports task-related activations. However, the tasks listed (motor clenching, internal speech, motor imagery) are not clearly problem-solving/reasoning tasks and participant demographics/health status are not specified. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

Study used H2 15O PET (positron emission tomography), not fMRI. The review requires task-based fMRI studies; although it involves healthy adults performing cognitive tasks and reports task-related activation, the imaging modality is non-fMRI, so it should be excluded.

This fMRI study investigates imitation of finger movements with goal-oriented actions, not problem-solving or reasoning. It does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task). The study is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activation (I1, I4), but it is not relevant to the review objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study of breath-holding/respiratory awareness, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). Although it involves human task fMRI and reports activation, the task domain is outside the review focus (problem solving).

This is not a task-based fMRI study: it uses PET and SPECT resting-state measurements in Parkinson’s disease patients (n=6). No healthy adult sample is included and the study is not fMRI nor task-based. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

Participants are healthy elderly (mean age 68.8), outside the 18–65 adult range required (E3). Although it is a task-based fMRI reporting activations (I1, I4), the sample does not include healthy adults aged 18–65; additionally the task is a 2-back working memory task rather than problem-solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract reports an fMRI study in perimenopausal/recently postmenopausal women examining prefrontal cognitive processes but does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task or clearly state task-based fMRI and healthy adult status. Insufficient information to assess I2 and I1/I3; include for full-text review.

The study uses a visual oddball attention/target-detection paradigm transferred from EEG to fMRI, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). Although it is task-based fMRI and reports BOLD activation, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.

The study is a task-based fMRI study in humans that reports task-related BOLD activation during visual discrimination tasks. However, the abstract does not indicate the tasks are problem-solving/reasoning nor does it give participant ages, so inclusion for full-text review is warranted to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a task-based fMRI study of episodic memory (object/position encoding and recognition), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the review's requirement for problem-solving tasks (I2).
The study reports data from epileptic patients (intracranial EEG) and does not include a healthy adult sample, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 (exclusion E3).
This is a PET study of hypnosis (not task-based fMRI) and does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks in the context of fMRI. Excluded due to non-fMRI modality (E4).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of emotional processing, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. It does not meet the meta-analysis’ task requirement from the abstract, but this is not an explicit exclusion criterion; include for full-text review to confirm whether any relevant problem-solving component was used.

This study used H2(15)O PET (regional cerebral blood flow) rather than fMRI, so it is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study and does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement. It also does not report problem-solving task fMRI results.

This is a PET (15O-water) study rather than fMRI, so it fails the modality requirement for the review. The tasks are motor-learning/reaching, not problem-solving/reasoning. Primary exclusion is non-fMRI modality (E4).
Study uses resting-state fMRI to examine network small-worldness in Alzheimer’s disease, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study in healthy adults.

Abstract discusses humor, smiling, and functional imaging but does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task, task-based fMRI methodology, or participant sample details. Insufficient information to determine whether the study meets the meta-analysis criteria; include for full-text review.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in right-handed schizophrenia patients assessing language lateralization; it does not mention any healthy adult sample or a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus it fails the requirement for a healthy adult sample and is not relevant to problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

This study used PET (H2[15]O) imaging, not fMRI, so it does not meet the modality requirement for inclusion in an fMRI meta-analysis. Participants and task are appropriate, but the non-fMRI modality clearly excludes it.

The study uses task-based fMRI of auditory monitoring and verbal working memory (mPASAT), not a problem-solving or reasoning task as required by the review. While it includes healthy adult controls and reports activation, it does not meet I2.
This is an fMRI study of musical emotion (passive listening/memorization), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow, not a task-based fMRI study. Therefore it fails the fMRI inclusion requirement (E4).

This is an EEG/fMRI case report of a patient with absence seizures examining resting-state BOLD changes and spike-and-wave discharges. It is not a task-based problem-solving study and includes no healthy adult participants.

The fMRI task is a syntactic sentence-judgment (language processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. Abstract clearly reports task-based fMRI with healthy adults and task-related activations (I1, I3, I4).

Study uses alcohol cue exposure in alcoholic subjects and social drinkers rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task; does not address the review objective of task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.
Imaging modality is H2[15]O-PET (not fMRI), so the study does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement. Exclude per E4.

Abstract is a theoretical/methodological proposal combining fMRI, EEG and MEG; it does not report an empirical task-based fMRI study, contains no human participants or problem-solving task, and provides no task-related activation results. Therefore it does not meet inclusion for empirical task-based fMRI problem-solving studies.

Resting-state fMRI study in overweight children (8–11 years) with no task-based problem-solving paradigm and no healthy adult participants. Excluded per E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The task is irony comprehension (social cognition) in schizophrenia, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult controls and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

The study investigates action observation/imagery for motor rehabilitation and reports corticomotor activation; it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults, so it fails inclusion criterion I2 (task type). Abstract gives no indication of problem-solving paradigms.

The study used resting-state fMRI (rs-fMRI) to relate intrinsic brain activity to semantic/ conceptual behavior and did not report task-evoked, task-based fMRI activation results. Although behavioral tasks were administered, imaging was not task-based. Meets exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.
This is a PET study (not task-based fMRI) and examines autobiographical future/past thinking rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement for this review.
Participants are adolescents (age 14) only; no healthy adult (18-65) sample is included, so the study does not meet the adult-sample inclusion criterion (I3).

Study used H2(15)O PET (not task-based fMRI), therefore fails the fMRI modality requirement. Additionally, tasks are semantic/episodic memory rather than problem-solving, so it does not address the review objective.
The fMRI task investigates attention/visual awareness during binocular suppression, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks (fails inclusion criterion I2).
Study uses task-based fMRI of masked emotional face perception, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not address the review objective (I2 not met).

Abstract reports regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) functional neuroimaging, consistent with PET/SPECT rather than fMRI, and investigates working memory in ADHD. The study therefore appears to be a non-fMRI imaging study and does not address problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Excluded because it is not a task-based fMRI study (E4).

The study examines memory-related default network activity in patients with multiple sclerosis, not task-based problem solving in healthy adults. The abstract indicates a clinical MS sample rather than healthy adult participants, so it does not meet the healthy adult sample criterion.

Focuses on ALS patients and motor-network plasticity related to disease progression; abstract gives no indication of a problem-solving/reasoning task or a healthy adult sample. Not relevant to task-based fMRI studies of problem solving in healthy adults.

This is a PET study in conscious rhesus monkeys using a pharmacological challenge; it is non-human and uses PET (not fMRI). Therefore it lacks healthy adult human participants and is not an fMRI study of problem solving.
Study uses structural MRI and PET with machine-learning; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement for this review.

This is a PET study of awakening/alertness, not an fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task and uses H2(15)O PET rather than fMRI.

The study examines real and imagined locomotion (walking), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Although it uses task-based fMRI and healthy adult participants and reports activations, it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving), so exclude.

The study used arterial spin labeling MRI to measure regional cerebral blood flow at rest after drug administration in healthy volunteers; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, meeting exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state study).

Abstract reports task-related cerebral activation/deactivation during active navigation in healthy volunteers and patient groups, but it does not explicitly state fMRI modality, whether navigation is a problem-solving/reasoning task per review criteria, or the ages of healthy participants. Insufficient detail — include for full-text review.
Study uses motor imagery/execution (tongue, finger, toe) — not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not address the review objective (I2 not met).

The study uses task-based BOLD fMRI in amnestic MCI patients performing an n-back task, but does not include a healthy adult sample (18–65). The review seeks task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults, so exclusion is appropriate.

This is a task-based fMRI study of jaw-tapping (a motor task) in healthy adults that examines activation in memory/cognitive regions; it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2), so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study and reports task-related BOLD activation/reliability, but does not explicitly state the task is a problem-solving/reasoning paradigm or clearly confirm a healthy adult sample. Include for full-text review to determine relevance.

The study focuses on functional connectivity measures (temporal correlations of BOLD) during working memory tasks rather than reporting task-related activation for a problem-solving/reasoning task. This fits the exclusion for connectivity-only studies.

Study is a clinical imaging study (MRI, rCBF, rMRGlu) in patients with cerebral microangiopathy, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. No healthy adult sample and not fMRI-based; thus it meets exclusion criteria.

The study used simultaneous task-based fMRI with a battery of cognitive tasks, meeting I1. The abstract does not specify that tasks were problem-solving/reasoning (I2) or participant ages/health status (I3). Insufficient information to confirm I2/I3, so include for full-text review.

The study uses a go/no-go response inhibition task rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations (meets I1, I3, I4), but does not address the review objective.
The study used PET imaging in non-human primates (monkeys), not task-based fMRI in healthy adult humans. It therefore does not include a healthy adult human sample and is not an fMRI study.

The task involved passive viewing of social vs. non-social images, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. The study is task-based fMRI with healthy controls and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 met), but does not address problem solving.

Study examines fMRI activation in HIV-1 positive patients (cognitively asymptomatic) during working memory tasks; the abstract does not report a healthy adult (18–65) sample or a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it fails the requirement for a healthy adult sample.

This is a PET (H2¹⁵O) study, not an fMRI study, and it examines motor imagery/execution rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and does not address the review task domain.

This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) during a working memory task following physostigmine infusion; it is not an fMRI study and does not investigate problem-solving/reasoning. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement.

This is a PET study of thirst/cerebellar responses, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. Although it includes healthy adults and reports activation, it uses a non-fMRI modality and does not involve problem-solving tasks. Excluded based on E4 (Non-fMRI studies).

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activation. However, the task is task-switching/intentional cognitive flexibility, not explicitly described as a problem-solving or reasoning task; include for full-text review to confirm relevance.

This study used H2(15)O PET (positron emission tomography), not task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement (E4). The task is a cognitive set-shifting visual discrimination, but the imaging modality excludes it.

This study used H2^15O PET rather than fMRI (non-fMRI modality) and investigated tactile discrimination/imagery rather than problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement for the review.
Non-human PET study in rhesus monkeys (not humans); uses PET imaging rather than fMRI and does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm in healthy adult humans.
This is a PET imaging study in Alzheimer's patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It lacks an fMRI modality and healthy adult sample.
The study uses interoceptive (heartbeat awareness) and cardiovascular arousal tasks rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

The study is a resting-state/functional connectivity analysis of spontaneous BOLD fluctuations under propofol (no task). It does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task and is not a task-based fMRI study, so it does not meet the review objective.

The study focuses on resting-state functional connectivity mapping (fcMRI) and comparisons to task-based maps for sensorimotor/visual/language/auditory tasks; it does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (connectivity/resting-state focus) and does not satisfy the review’s requirement for problem-solving tasks (I2).

The study reports phonological and semantic language tasks, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting activations but is not relevant to problem solving in healthy adults.

This study examines guilt-related script-driven imagery (an emotion task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It appears to be task-based fMRI and reports activations, but it is not relevant to problem-solving in healthy adults.

Study reports task-based fMRI of cue-induced craving in cocaine-dependent women; participants are clinical (not healthy adults) and the task is drug-cue exposure, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Excluded because there is no healthy adult sample.

This is a task-based event-related fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation (memory search). The abstract does not clearly indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task nor specify participant ages/health. Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the review definition of problem solving and includes healthy adults.

The study investigates emotional awareness and dACC responses to emotional stimuli (viewing pictures), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. The abstract reports regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) measures (consistent with PET), not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review objective and appears to be a non-fMRI study.

The study uses a reading paradigm and reports correlations between MEG and fMRI signals rather than task-based activation in a problem-solving or reasoning task. It does not address problem-solving in healthy adults and focuses on cross-modal correlational analyses (connectivity-type), so it is not eligible for the review.

Event-related fMRI study manipulating referential ambiguity during sentence comprehension; authors explicitly state readers engaged in 'problem-solving' to select referents and report BOLD activation patterns. Participant age/health not specified in abstract, so full-text needed to confirm I3. Include for full-text review.

Study is an animal (rat) resting-state BOLD fMRI study under propofol anesthesia, not a task-based fMRI of problem solving in humans. No healthy adult human participants and no problem-solving task are included, so it meets exclusion criteria.
The study used H2(15)O-PET (a non-fMRI modality) and investigated neural responses to phobic stimuli, not problem-solving tasks. Thus it fails the fMRI requirement (E4).

The study uses a working memory (WM) cognitive task and BOLD calibration but does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Although it is task-based fMRI in (likely) healthy adults reporting activation, it does not meet the required I2 criterion (problem-solving/reasoning).

The study uses a picture memory encoding task (memory), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adult controls and reports activation (meets I1, I3, I4).

Study uses task-based fMRI of amputees and controls performing virtual/imagined limb movements, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). Although it meets I1, I3, and I4, the task is motor imagery, so it is outside the review scope.

The study uses prosaccade/antisaccade oculomotor tasks (cognitive stability/plasticity), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (does not meet I2). It is a task-based fMRI study of healthy volunteers and reports task-related activation (I1, I4), but the task domain is outside the review scope.

The study uses PET imaging (H1 receptor occupancy and rCBF) rather than fMRI. The review requires task-based fMRI studies of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the non-fMRI exclusion criterion.

The study uses fMRI in healthy adults and reports task activation, but the tasks are emotional face matching, reward anticipation and an n-back working memory task — none are problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

Task involves phoneme and timbre identification in a musical priming paradigm, not problem solving or reasoning; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting activations but does not address the review objective.

Task-based fMRI in 16 healthy young adults reporting task-related BOLD activation following flavanol-rich cocoa. The abstract describes a cognitive task but does not specify that it is a problem-solving or reasoning task, so include for full-text review to determine task type.

The study uses a GO–NOGO sustained attention/response inhibition task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). It is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activation, but does not address problem solving.

This is a PET study of pain processing using thermal stimuli in healthy volunteers, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and does not involve problem-solving tasks.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in healthy younger and older adults and task-related BOLD signal (variability) differences between fixation and task, but it does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task or exact adult age ranges. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

The study investigates action observation/imagery/execution of reaching movements, not problem solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task) despite being a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activations.

Study recruited chronic smokers (no healthy adult sample specified) and the task is an N-back working memory task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task; therefore it does not meet the review objective.

Although a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations, participants observed others searching rather than performing a problem‑solving or reasoning task themselves. Therefore it does not meet the review’s required inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning task).

This is a clinical genetic study of hereditary spastic paraplegia in affected family members; it does not report task-based fMRI data (only PET in one patient) and does not include a healthy adult sample performing problem-solving tasks.

Study uses language/syntactic processing tasks (sentence verification, plausibility, non-word detection), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations, but it does not meet the review’s required I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This fMRI study examines gain/loss expectation and reward processing in mania (clinical sample), not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. The study likely focuses on patients with mania and does not address the review’s target (task-based problem solving in healthy adults).

This is a resting-state fMRI study linking spontaneous BOLD fluctuations to pupil diameter; it does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This study used H2(15)O PET (not fMRI) and examined patients in a persistent vegetative state rather than healthy adults; it does not involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adult participants. Excluded based on non-fMRI modality and lack of healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes analysis of simulated and experimental block-design (task-based) fMRI data, but does not specify that the behavioral tasks involved problem-solving/reasoning or that participants were healthy adults aged 18–65. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3; include for full-text review.

Study examines resting-state effects of sevoflurane on rCBF and BOLD in normal subjects, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study. It reports resting-state/anesthesia comparisons, meeting the resting-state exclusion criterion.

This is a narrative review of brown adipose tissue imaging, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in humans. It is a review article (E1) and does not report fMRI task activation data (E4).

The study involves prostate cancer patients receiving androgen deprivation therapy; no indication of a healthy adult control group or healthy sample. Thus it fails inclusion criterion I3 (healthy adult sample).

This is a task-based BOLD fMRI study in healthy adult social drinkers reporting task-related brain activation. The task is a Stroop conflict/inhibition task; the abstract does not clearly indicate a problem-solving or reasoning paradigm, so include for full-text review to confirm relevance.

Abstract does not clearly state this is a task-based fMRI study or that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task (it describes saccadic conflict/error processing under intoxication). Insufficient information to determine I1 and I2 from the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm modality and task type.

Abstract reports longitudinal resting-state regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) changes related to an Alzheimer’s risk variant; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. This is a resting-state imaging study, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of reward processing, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. It therefore does not address the meta-analysis objective (problem solving in healthy adults).

This study investigates resting-state/default mode network connectivity during propofol sedation rather than a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI paradigm. It is a resting-state/connectivity-focused study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract does not specify imaging modality, whether a task-based fMRI was used, whether a problem-solving/reasoning task was employed, or whether healthy adult participants were included. Insufficient information to apply inclusion/exclusion criteria; include for full-text review.

Task-switching (cued task switching) is an executive control paradigm, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is a task-based BOLD fMRI study with healthy adults and reports task-related activation (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it is not relevant to the review objective.

The study uses emotion-induction narratives and examines somatosensory responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports activation, but it is not relevant to the review objective of problem-solving.

The study uses task-based fMRI to examine emotional appraisal with false physiological feedback, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does involve task fMRI and reports activation (I1, I4).

The task is picture-encoding (episodic memory), not a problem-solving/reasoning task, and participants are described as nondemented older adults (i.e., not the target healthy adult age range 18–65). Therefore no eligible healthy adult (18–65) problem-solving fMRI sample is present.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study with task-related BOLD activations (I1, I4). However, the tasks involve visual imagery/perception rather than problem-solving/reasoning (I2 not clearly met), and participant details/ages are not specified (I3 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm whether a relevant problem-solving task and healthy adult sample are present.

The study uses task-based fMRI and reports BOLD activation for speech comprehension, but the task is speech processing, not problem-solving or reasoning; it therefore does not meet the key inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving).

Abstract describes task-based fMRI in patients with new-onset systemic lupus erythematosus; no healthy adult control group is mentioned. Therefore it does not include healthy adults required for this review.

The study reports resting-state measures (regional homogeneity of spontaneous BOLD fluctuations and resting-state connectivity) from a combined intervention in older adults, not task-based fMRI during problem-solving. Participants are described as older adults (outside the 18–65 target). Thus it meets exclusion criteria for resting-state/connectivity-only (E2) and non-target age group (E3).

The study is a task-based fMRI of imagined locomotion (motor/kinesthetic imagery), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet the review’s focus (I2). It does include healthy adult controls and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), but the core task is not problem solving.

This is a computational/theoretical modeling study of cortical dynamics; no human participants or task-based fMRI data, and it does not report task-related brain activation in healthy adults. Does not meet inclusion criteria I1–I4.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult controls and task-related activation, but the cognitive task is described as a challenging working memory/fatigue task rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task. Insufficient information in abstract to confirm it meets the problem-solving/reasoning criterion, so include for full-text review.

Study used PET ([15O]butanol) not task-based fMRI, so it fails the modality requirement. Although it includes a cognitive (maze) task and likely healthy adults, the paper is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study and therefore should be excluded.

This is a PET (H215O) study of face-matching/object perception, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET rather than fMRI, so it fails the non-fMRI exclusion criterion.

Study is a PET imaging experiment in conscious rhesus monkeys (non-human subjects) and not an fMRI study of humans performing problem-solving tasks. Does not include healthy adult human participants or task-based fMRI data relevant to the review.
Study used PET in rhesus monkeys (non-human) and is not an fMRI study; therefore it does not include healthy adult humans nor task-based fMRI data.
This is a task-based fMRI study of passive median nerve electrical stimulation (sensory activation) in healthy volunteers, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This study used PET ([H2(15)O] positron emission tomography), not fMRI, and the task was an attentional sustained-attention task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement.

The task is spatial memory/navigation rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review objective (does not meet I2). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy controls (meets I1, I3, I4) but fails the problem-solving criterion.

Abstract reports task-based BOLD fMRI in healthy adult volunteers and task-related activation results (working memory/executive task). However, it does not explicitly state a problem-solving or reasoning task. Include for full-text review to verify task type.
Study used PET (not fMRI) to measure brain activity, so it is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study and does not meet the fMRI requirement.

Study used a simple sustained-attention (Rapid Visual Information Processing) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). It is a task-based fMRI study of healthy adults reporting activations, but the cognitive domain does not match the meta-analysis objective.
Study uses nonhuman primates (marmoset model); no human healthy adult participants and not a problem-solving task. Fails inclusion criterion I3 and meets exclusion criterion E3.

Resting-state positron-emission tomography (not task-based fMRI) and no task/problem-solving paradigm; does not report task-related activation. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for resting-state studies and non-fMRI modality.

The study reports resting-state functional connectivity analyses rather than a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving or reasoning. It does not involve participants performing a problem-solving task, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study and reports task-related brain activation results. However, it does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor specify participant age/health. Include for full-text review to determine whether I2 and I3 are met.
This is a PET study of propofol-induced loss of consciousness, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task and uses non-fMRI imaging (PET).

The study used a GO/NOGO response-inhibition task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (does not meet I2). It is task-based fMRI with healthy controls, but the task domain is outside scope.

Study is a task-based fMRI with 34 healthy adult participants and reports task-related brain activation. However, the tasks described (Stroop and self-referential processing) are not clearly problem-solving or reasoning paradigms. Abstract does not provide sufficient evidence that inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning task) is met, so include for full-text review to confirm.

Abstract reports a task-based block-design fMRI study that characterizes transient, task-related BOLD responses across four tasks, and reports spatial activation profiles. However, it does not specify that the tasks involved problem-solving/reasoning or provide participant demographic details. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 (problem-solving task) and I3 (healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes fMRI of visual/emotional processing during an attention task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy subjects and reports activation, it does not meet the review’s required I2 criterion.

The study uses an n-back working memory task with schematic facial expressions, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). It is a task-based fMRI study reporting activations (meets I1 and I4) but does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

While this is a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving (Tower of London) and reports task-related activations, all participants are breast cancer survivors (chemo vs non-chemo). There is no healthy adult (18–65) group, so it fails the requirement for a healthy adult sample.

This is a commentary and simulation addressing MVPA methodology, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. The abstract reports no human participants performing problem-solving tasks or task-related activation results, so it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

Abstract describes brain responses to visceral/somatic stimuli in patients with IBS and fibromyalgia, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, and appears to involve patient groups rather than healthy adult sample. Outside review scope.

The study uses awake macaque monkeys (non-human primates) and investigates visual motion processing, not problem-solving or reasoning in human adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult human sample (I3) nor address the review task (I2).

Task-based fMRI using the Multi-Source Interference Task (MSIT) with reported task-related BOLD responses in a healthy subject population. It is unclear from the abstract whether MSIT meets the review's specific definition of 'problem-solving or reasoning'; include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

This fMRI study examines neural responses to coaching/mentoring (PEA vs NEA), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although task-based and reports activations, it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This is a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related brain activation during visuospatial planning (a problem-solving task) and emotional encoding. The study includes a healthy control group, so it meets inclusion criteria I1–I4.

Study used task-based fMRI in schizophrenia patients (n=6) performing listening to hallucinated words, not a problem-solving/reasoning task, and does not include a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet inclusion requirements.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI. Although it involves human participants performing reasoning tasks and reports activations, its imaging modality is non-fMRI, so excluded.

Event-related task-based fMRI study using a problem-solving task (Tower of Hanoi) and reporting task-related brain activations. Abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state healthy adult sample, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This study used PET imaging ([15O]H2O) with pharmacological challenge (d-fenfluramine), not task-based fMRI, and does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus it does not meet the fMRI task-based criteria.

Animal study using lactating rats (dams); no human adult participants and not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Although task-based fMRI was used, the study fails inclusion criteria I2 and I3 and clearly meets exclusion E3.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adult volunteers reporting task-related BOLD activations. However, the tasks described are emotion processing, working memory, and attentional control—not explicitly problem-solving/reasoning. Insufficient detail to confirm presence of a problem-solving task, so include for full-text review to verify I2.

Abstract indicates a task-based functional neuroimaging study in healthy adults reporting task-related brain activity during the n-back task, but it is unclear whether the n-back (working memory) meets the review’s 'problem-solving or reasoning' task definition. Include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI pipeline optimization and reports task-related activation, but it does not specify that the tasks involve problem solving or the participant age/health. Insufficient information to confirm problem-solving tasks or adult healthy sample, so include for full-text review.

This study uses motor imagery (kinesthetic movement imagination), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not address the review objective. It focuses on EEG–fMRI correlation of oscillatory activity rather than task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

The task is auditory imagery of hearing a monotone, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). It is task-based fMRI and reports activations (I1, I4), but does not address the review objective.

This is a PET study (H2 15O), not an fMRI study, and the tasks involve gesture pantomime/imitation/action semantics rather than problem-solving or reasoning. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem-solving tasks.
