The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of subliminal visuomotor priming (masked prime reaction-time task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It includes healthy adults and reports task-related activations, but fails inclusion criterion I2 (no problem-solving/reasoning task).

The abstract focuses on modulation of functional connectivity (steady-state connectivity during N-back and resting state) and does not report task-related activation results. This is a connectivity/resting-state-focused study and meets exclusion E2.

This fMRI study investigates emotional olfactory processing (odor-induced mood) in schizophrenia patients, relatives, and healthy controls, not a problem‑solving or reasoning task (fails I2). It meets I1, I3, and I4 but does not address the review objective, so excluded.

This is an animal (rat) pharmacological fMRI study; no human participants or healthy adult sample are reported, and it does not involve human problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review population criteria.

This study investigates breast cancer survivors (clinical sample) 5–10 years after chemotherapy and evaluates cerebral blood flow/metabolism rather than task-based fMRI during a problem-solving task. It therefore does not include healthy adult problem-solving fMRI data needed for the review.

This randomized trial examines CBT effects on cognitive reappraisal in social anxiety disorder patients — an emotion-regulation task in a clinical sample. It does not investigate problem-solving in healthy adults and appears to lack a healthy adult sample.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study in normal volunteers performing mental calculation (a form of problem solving), with task-related BOLD activation results reported. No exclusion criteria apply.

This study uses resting-state intrinsic BOLD functional connectivity to define brain networks, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving or reasoning. It does not involve participants performing a problem-solving task, so it does not meet the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study examining BOLD responses during algebraic and symbol-manipulation problem-solving tasks; reports region-specific task-related activations. Abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health status, so include for full-text verification of healthy adult sample.

The study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations but investigates emotion recognition (happy/sad) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. Other exclusion criteria (E1–E4) do not apply.

The task is a target-detection/financial-reward and motor task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. The study is task-based fMRI and reports activation, but it does not meet the review’s core requirement of a problem-solving task in healthy adults.

This is a task-based fMRI study of trauma script-driven imagery/emotional awareness in trauma-exposed individuals (with and without PTSD), not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It does not meet the review’s key inclusion (I2) and does not clearly provide a healthy adult problem-solving sample.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of metamemory (judgments of learning) and face–name associative memory, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. Although it reports task-related fMRI activation, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults (I2 not met).

Imaging modality is PET, not fMRI. Although the study uses human participants on problem-solving tasks and includes healthy control adults with reported task-related activation, it is a non-fMRI study and therefore fails the fMRI inclusion criterion.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants reporting task-related activations (meets I1, I3, I4). However, the task is a phonological/reading judgment rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task (likely fails I2), so full-text review is recommended to confirm relevance.

Abstract describes EEG/ERP methodology and interpretation (single-trial amplitudes, phase-locking) and appears to be a methodological/review paper rather than a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. Not fMRI and likely a review.

The abstract describes fMRI mapping in chronic intractable epilepsy (clinical patient population) and does not mention healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks. It therefore does not meet the requirement to include a healthy adult sample (I3).

The task is masked emotional face perception (sad/happy), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. Although it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not address the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related BOLD activation. However, the task involves working memory of musical sounds rather than explicit problem-solving/reasoning; include for full-text review to determine whether the task meets the review's problem-solving definition.

The study examines action observation and imagery (mirror neuron system), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Although it is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation (I1 and I4), the task content is outside the review scope.

Abstract describes abstinence-induced working memory alterations in smokers (a nicotine-dependent sample) and genetic associations; it does not address problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor indicate inclusion of a healthy adult control group. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.
This is a PET study of consciousness under anesthesia, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor use fMRI methods.

The study investigates attentional modulation of emotional stimulus processing (emotional expectancy and pictures), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It is a task-based fMRI study in humans reporting activation, but it does not meet the review’s required cognitive domain (I2).

The study uses a face/house discrimination paradigm (fMRI+ERP), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (satisfies I1 and I4), but does not involve problem solving in healthy adults as defined by the review objective, so excluded from this review.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation. It is unclear from the abstract whether the task qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task and participant ages are not specified; include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is an animal (rat) fMRI study of sensory responses under anesthesia, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in human adults. The meta-analysis requires healthy adult human participants performing problem-solving tasks, so the study is excluded.

While this is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation, the task is spatial imagery/mental scanning (haptic imagery), not a problem-solving or reasoning task as required by the review. The abstract does not describe problem solving or reasoning processes, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

The study uses task-based fMRI but investigates synaesthesia/olfactory perception (single-case and controls) and does not involve any problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet the review objective (I2).

Study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it is a non-fMRI study (E4). Additionally, the task was recognition memory for food items, not a problem-solving/reasoning task, so it does not meet the review objective.

The study uses fMRI to map speech comprehension (language) tasks, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks; thus it does not address the review objective. Abstract indicates task-based fMRI with activation results but not the required problem-solving paradigm in healthy adults.
This study used rTMS with PET to measure regional cerebral blood flow, not task-based fMRI, and does not investigate problem-solving tasks. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement for inclusion.

Sample consists of 14-year-old adolescents (N=54), not healthy adults aged 18–65. Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting activation, participants are not within the adult age range required by the review, so it must be excluded.
The study used PET (not task-based fMRI) and involved Alzheimer’s disease patients rather than healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement or the healthy adult sample requirement.

The task is spoken sentence comprehension (language processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (does not meet inclusion criterion I2). The study is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations (I1, I4 satisfied), but it does not address problem solving.

Study uses rectal distention/gut stimulation to probe autonomic/interoceptive responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it appears to be task-based fMRI and reports brain activation (I1, I4), it does not address the review objective (no problem-solving task).

Abstract reports task-based BOLD fMRI related to developing gestural fluency (so I1 and I4 appear met). However it does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2) nor that participants are healthy adults aged 18–65 (I3). Insufficient information to confirm those, so include for full-text review.

Task-based BOLD fMRI in healthy adult males reporting task-related activation (satisfies I1, I3, I4). However the task is described as a working memory paradigm, not explicitly a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the review's problem-solving/reasoning definition.

Abstract describes a working-memory manipulation task (manipulation vs. maintenance) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 met). No exclusion criteria (E1–E4) clearly apply.

Study examines youth with heavy prenatal alcohol exposure (FASD), not healthy adults (18–65). Although it is a task-based fMRI reporting task-related activation (spatial working memory), the population does not meet the healthy adult sample criterion required by the review.

This is a resting-state fMRI study examining connectivity changes during propofol-induced loss of consciousness; it does not report a task-based problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore meets the resting-state/connectivity exclusion.

This is a PET study (not fMRI) examining object recognition rather than problem solving. It therefore does not meet the meta-analysis requirement for task-based fMRI (I1) or a problem-solving task (I2). Excluded based on E4 (Non-fMRI study).

The study samples healthy elders and clinical groups (a-MCI, mild AD) rather than healthy adults in the 18–65 age range required by the review; the abstract does not indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the required healthy adult sample criterion.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation during a decision-making task in human participants. The abstract shows encoding, manipulation, and neural activation results but does not report participant ages or clearly label the task as problem-solving; insufficient detail to exclude, so include for full-text review.
Study used PET (not fMRI), included only cocaine-dependent men (no healthy adult group), and the task was anger imagery rather than problem-solving/reasoning. These meet exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

The study investigates resting-state low-frequency BOLD correlations modulated by recent visual experience (connectivity/resting-state focus), not task-based problem solving or reasoning. It is a connectivity/resting-state study rather than a task-based problem-solving fMRI study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related BOLD activation, but it does not clearly state that participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task nor does it specify participant age/health. Because key inclusion items (I2, I3) are uncertain from the abstract, include for full-text review.

Abstract reports task-based event-related fMRI with task-related BOLD activation during emotional and non-emotional judgment of IAPS images. It does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving or reasoning task nor provide participant ages/health status. Include for full-text review to confirm whether I2 (problem-solving/reasoning) and I3 (healthy adults 18-65) are met.
Study participants are adolescents (binge-drinking adolescents); no healthy adult (18–65) sample is reported, so it fails the adult-sample inclusion criterion.

The study used a multisensory Stroop task assessing cognitive control/inhibition, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adult controls and reports task activation (I1, I3, I4), but fails I2.
The study used PET (not fMRI) and examined motor imagery of locomotor tasks rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet the review's fMRI/problem-solving criteria.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of language discourse comprehension and memory, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the review objective (no evidence of a problem-solving task).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study using mental calculation and reports activated brain areas/BOLD results. Participant age/health not reported, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

The study used task-based fMRI and includes healthy controls and activation results (I1, I3, I4). However the task is a working memory rehearsal (Sternberg) paradigm, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet I2 (problem-solving). Exclude based on not meeting required task domain.

The fMRI task involves auditory perception of material properties, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet I2. It is task-based and includes healthy adults, but the study does not address problem solving.

The study uses task-based fMRI of tool-use and motor imagery, not a problem-solving or reasoning task — it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does include healthy adults and reports task activations, but the tasks are motor/tool-use, not problem solving.

The study investigates motor imagery and motor recovery in stroke patients using task-based fMRI, not problem-solving or reasoning in healthy adults. There is no healthy adult sample reported, so it fails the requirement for healthy participants.

Abstract describes benzodiazepine-induced amnesia and memory impairment; no mention of task-based fMRI, problem-solving/reasoning tasks, or healthy adult fMRI results. Therefore excluded as a non-fMRI study (E4).

Study used PET (H2(15)O), not fMRI, and examined motor imagery rather than problem-solving/reasoning. Although it included healthy subjects, the imaging modality is non-fMRI, so it fails the inclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study in patients with major depressive disorder performing a Stroop cognitive control task; the abstract does not report a healthy adult sample (18–65). It therefore fails the requirement for inclusion of healthy adults.

The study examines classical conditioning of sexual arousal and neural correlates, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning task).

Abstract describes a review article of neuroimaging in Parkinson’s disease cognitive deficits, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria for being a review and for not including a healthy adult sample performing problem-solving tasks.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing a probabilistic reasoning/problem-solving task ("Jumping to Conclusions"); reports analysis of task-related BOLD activation in prefrontal subregions. Not a review, not resting-state, and uses fMRI.

The study used PET (regional cerebral blood flow) rather than fMRI, which meets exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI studies). Although it involves a mental rotation task likely in healthy adults and reports activation, the imaging modality disqualifies it for this fMRI meta-analysis.

The study examines action observation and actor identity using fMRI videos, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). The abstract also does not specify participant age/health to confirm a healthy adult sample (I3 unclear).

Study used PET (H2 15O) rather than fMRI, sample consists of abstinent opiate-dependent subjects (no healthy adult group), and the paper focuses on functional connectivity analysis. These meet exclusion criteria E4, E3, and E2.

Abstract reports rCBF changes during emotional image viewing (emotional perception), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It does not specify fMRI and describes rCBF measurement (non-fMRI), so it fails the review objective and meets exclusion E4.
This is a PET study of PTSD patients; it is not an fMRI study and does not report task-based fMRI in healthy adults. Therefore it fails inclusion (no healthy adult sample and non-fMRI modality).

This study used H2^15O positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI, so it fails the imaging modality criterion (E4). Additionally, the task is motor imagery/kinesthetic illusion rather than problem-solving, so it does not address the review objective, but primary exclusion is non-fMRI modality.

Study uses simultaneous EEG–fMRI with a forced-choice reaction/auditory attention task (effortful decision making), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based, includes healthy adults, and reports task-related activations, it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it is excluded.

This is a PET study in Parkinson's disease patients examining motor execution/imagery and effects of STN stimulation; it is not task-based fMRI, does not involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria.

Task is fear conditioning (emotional learning), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Study is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activation (I1, I4), but it does not address the review objective of problem solving.

Abstract reports task-based event-related fMRI with task-related activation but uses an English verb generation (language) task, which is not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task. Abstract lacks explicit participant age/health details. Include for full-text review to determine whether task meets the review's problem-solving criterion.

The task is autobiographical irritability induction (emotional provocation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet I2. Abstract does describe task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4).

The task is emotion regulation (viewing negative images and cognitive reappraisal), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations.

Title/abstract focus on depressed patients' causal attribution; no indication of any healthy adult (18-65) comparison group or inclusion. Also the task is about attribution in depression rather than problem-solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review population/objective.

The study uses task-based fMRI of prayer, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does report task-related activations (I1, I4) but does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

This is a case study of reading-induced seizures in epilepsy patients using EEG-fMRI and MEG; it does not study problem-solving or reasoning tasks nor include healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet the review’s population or task criteria.

Study investigates auditory processing in minimally conscious and vegetative-state patients (severely brain injured), not healthy adults and not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it lacks a healthy adult sample and does not address problem-solving tasks.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study showing altered neural activation during executive cognition, but does not specify inclusion of healthy adult participants or an explicit problem-solving/reasoning task. Insufficient detail to exclude; include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI with healthy adult control group and task-related activation results are reported. However, the SRS task assesses inhibitory attentional control and it is unclear whether this qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task; include for full-text review to confirm I2.

The study examines syntactic/language processing (relative clauses, plausibility judgments), not problem-solving or reasoning. Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adult subjects and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), it fails to meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it should be excluded.

The task is a hand laterality/motor imagery judgment, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4 present), but it does not address problem solving.

The study used awake-behaving macaque (nonhuman primate) participants and did not include any human healthy adult (18-65) sample. The task (photic stimulation) is not a human problem-solving task. Excluded based on no healthy adult sample.

This is a PET study (H2[15]O), not an fMRI study, and the task involves emotional facial expressions/laughter induced by comics rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI or task-type inclusion criteria.

This is a PET study (not fMRI), so it does not meet the fMRI requirement. Additionally, the tasks are internal reading/visual imagery rather than problem-solving, but the primary exclusion is that the modality is non-fMRI.

Modeling/theoretical paper focused on neurovascular/metabolic coupling in gliomas; no human participants or healthy adult sample and not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, so it does not meet the review criteria.

The study used PET to measure regional cerebral blood flow (non-fMRI), so it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the fMRI requirement. It also focuses on verbal creativity rather than explicit problem-solving tasks. Main exclusion: non-fMRI modality.

The study investigates motor imagery (single-hand) using fMRI and quantifies BOLD power differences; it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review objective. Although it is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

Task-based fMRI with healthy adult control group and task-related activation reported. However, the tasks are executive-function/attention paradigms, not clearly described as problem-solving or reasoning; abstract provides insufficient information to determine whether I2 is met, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes a randomized hormone therapy trial assessing cognitive performance in early postmenopausal women; it does not report any task-based fMRI, problem-solving fMRI paradigm, or task-related brain activation. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.

Study used PET (not fMRI) to measure regional cerebral blood flow and examined spatial/topographical memory encoding in a virtual environment rather than problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement for this review.

The study uses a Go–NoGo inhibition task (not a problem-solving or reasoning task), so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI and reports BOLD responses, but the task mismatch excludes it from this review focused on problem-solving.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activations in healthy adult volunteers (n=18) performing a rule-guided response selection task, which qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task (implementation of arbitrary rules). All required inclusion criteria are met.

This study examines resting-state intrinsic vs extrinsic awareness networks using resting-state-like behavioral measures and task-free fMRI, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.

The study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults but investigates motor execution and motor imagery (neurofeedback), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore fails to meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This is a task-based fMRI study in young children focusing on auditory language comprehension, not problem-solving, and does not include healthy adults (18-65). It does not meet the review population or task criteria.

This is a PET study measuring cerebral blood flow, not task-based fMRI, and it examines perception and memory (implicit/explicit) for visual objects rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI modality criterion and does not address the review objective.
This study uses PET (not fMRI) and examines anger via script-driven imagery rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet imaging modality requirement for inclusion.

Study investigates emotional reactivity and emotion regulation in social anxiety during social/physical threat, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

The task described is emotional face/word processing (gender discrimination, case/syllable judgments), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 unmet). The study is task-based fMRI and reports activation, but does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

The study used task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants and reports task-related activation, but participants observed goal-directed actions rather than performing a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Study investigated abstinent cocaine-dependent participants performing inhibitory control tasks using fMRI, not problem-solving in healthy adults. No healthy adult sample is reported, so it does not meet the review population criterion.
This is a PET study investigating episodic memory encoding (not a problem-solving task) and is a non-fMRI neuroimaging modality, so it fails the fMRI requirement for inclusion.

This study used PET to measure brain activity during REM sleep, not task-based fMRI or a problem-solving/reasoning task in adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.

Study examines reward responses to food and prediction of body mass, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). Although it appears to be task-based fMRI (I1) and reports activation (I4), it is irrelevant to the review objective.

Abstract describes inhibitory control tasks (Stroop, anti-saccade) and a methods paper for correlating behavior with fMRI; it does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (I2 not met).

This is a task-based BOLD fMRI study reporting cue- and target-related ACC and DLPFC activation. However, the task is attentional control/task-switching rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task, and participant age/health are not specified in the abstract. Insufficient information to determine whether the task meets the review's 'problem solving' definition or whether sample is healthy adults — include for full-text review.
This is an animal (rat) fMRI study with no human participants; therefore it does not include healthy adult humans (exclusion E3).

The task is single-word comprehension (semantic similarity judgments), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI and reports BOLD activation, but the cognitive task is not problem solving in healthy adults.
This is a PET study (H2[15]O), not a task-based fMRI, and examines language/story listening rather than problem solving. Fails the fMRI requirement — exclusion E4 applies.

The study uses working memory and sustained attention tasks, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (I2 not met). It is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations, but does not address problem solving.

Study is a neuroimaging task study in abstinent smokers (working memory), not in a healthy adult sample and does not assess problem solving/reasoning. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult sample (E3) and is outside the review focus.

Tasks are lexical/linguistic decision tasks (conceptual, syntactic, morpho-phonological), not problem-solving or reasoning. Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 met), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it is not relevant to the review objective.
The study used PET (15O-H2O) rather than fMRI and examined episodic memory encoding (not problem-solving). Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem-solving tasks.

This study used PET (15O-butanol PET), not fMRI, and investigated olfactory/pheromone responses rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the task-based fMRI problem-solving inclusion and is a non-fMRI study.

This is an event-related fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task activations, but the experimental tasks are theory-of-mind and emotional sentence classification, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks; it fails inclusion criterion I2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of emotional reactivity and regulation (cognitive reappraisal), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). It does include healthy adults and reports task activation.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adult volunteers reporting task-related activation, but the task is a Stroop (conflict/inhibition) task rather than an explicit problem-solving/reasoning task. Unclear if this meets the review's definition of problem solving — include for full-text review to confirm.

Task-based fMRI study that reports task-related activations and task-modulated deactivation; however the abstract does not specify that the task is problem-solving/reasoning nor participant ages/health. Include for full-text review to determine I2 and I3.

Participants are children (ages 6–9) with TBI and orthopedic injuries; no healthy adult (18–65) sample is included. Thus the study does not meet the review’s requirement for healthy adult participants.
This is a task-based fMRI decoding study of object categories, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it does not meet the review’s required inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).
Study uses an auditory comprehension (language) task in high-risk offspring of schizophrenia, not a problem-solving/reasoning task in healthy adults; does not address the review objective.

Abstract describes a methodological Bayesian approach to HRF estimation in event-related fMRI but does not specify participant population, task type, or whether data involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Insufficient information to assess I2–I4; include for full-text review to determine relevance to problem-solving in healthy adults.
This study used PET measurement of regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF), not fMRI, so it is a non-fMRI imaging study and therefore excluded per E4 (Non-fMRI studies).

Task-based fMRI with healthy adults (ages 22–56) reporting task-related BOLD activation. However, the task is semantic verbal fluency (language production), which may not clearly meet the review’s ‘problem solving or reasoning’ criterion. Abstract provides sufficient methodological detail to include for full-text review to resolve task relevance.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI, which fails the review's imaging-modality requirement (E4). It also investigates sexual/competitive arousal rather than problem-solving tasks. Participants are healthy adults but the imaging modality excludes it.

The study uses rtfMRI for motor imagery to control a robotic arm, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults (fails I2).

The study investigates cue-induced brain responses to smoking cues in nicotine-dependent subjects, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Participants are nicotine-dependent (clinical sample), so it does not include a healthy adult sample as required.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study and reports spatio-temporal activity maps/classification (task-related brain signals), but it does not clearly indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task nor explicitly confirm a healthy adult sample. Include for full-text review to verify I2 and I3.

The task is semantic ambiguity/context verification (language processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet the problem-solving/reasoning criterion.

Study used PET (not fMRI), so it does not meet the modality requirement for this fMRI-focused review (E4). Additionally, the task is syntactic sentence processing rather than problem-solving/reasoning, so it does not address the review objective.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activation (I1, I3, I4), the behavioral task investigates working memory and spatial reference-frame shifting, not a problem-solving or reasoning task as required by the review (does not meet I2).

The task involves monitoring changes in auditory statistical regularities (event segmentation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet I2. The study is task-based fMRI and reports activation (I1, I4), but the task domain does not match the review objective.
The task is a memory retrieval paradigm (assessing encoding/recall and cognitive style), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
exclusion_criteria_applied: Does not meet I2 (task is memory retrieval, not problem solving)

screening_results.reason: Abstract describes real-time fMRI for sensory, motor, auditory, and language tasks but does not report problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It is therefore not relevant to the review objective (no evidence of problem-solving tasks in healthy adults).

Abstract reports task-related BOLD activation (fMRI) but does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task; provides insufficient information to confirm criterion I2. Include for full-text review to determine whether the fMRI task meets the ‘problem-solving/reasoning’ requirement.

Task-based BOLD fMRI study in healthy controls that reports task-related brain activation (working-memory task). It is unclear from the abstract whether the working-memory task meets the review's specific definition of a problem-solving or reasoning task, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study with (presumably) healthy adult participants and reports task-related brain activation. However, the task is speech perception/semantic context, not explicitly a problem-solving or reasoning task. Because it’s unclear whether the task meets the review’s definition of problem solving, include for full-text review.

The study uses task-based fMRI of episodic and working memory, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks (I2 not met). Although it includes healthy adult controls and reports task-related BOLD activation (I1, I3, I4), it does not address problem solving as required by the review.

Study uses PET imaging (regional cerebral blood flow/activity concentration), not fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement (E4). Additionally, the task described is language, not a problem-solving task, but exclusion is clear based on imaging modality.

This study is a resting-state fMRI study of elementary school children (not adults) and does not report a task-based problem-solving paradigm. It therefore fails criteria for task-based fMRI and adult healthy participants.

This is an fMRI study of emotional prosody identification, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning). Participants are healthy adults and task-related activations are reported, but the study does not address the review objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study of sentence comprehension (language processing and short-term memory), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). It reports activation in healthy adults, but the task domain does not match the meta-analysis objective.

The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adult readers and reports task-related activation (meets I1, I3, I4), but the task is semantic categorization/language anomaly detection, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI with task-related activation but uses an n-back working memory task; it is unclear from the abstract whether this qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task for the review and ages of participants are not specified. Include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Task-based fMRI study reporting GLM-derived activation measures during cognitive tasks (Stroop variant and rotating L's). Abstract does not explicitly state these are problem-solving tasks nor confirm participants are healthy adults. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

Participants are habitual smokers undergoing short-term cessation with no indication of a healthy adult control group, so the study lacks a healthy adult sample (E3). Additionally, the task used (rapid visual information processing) evaluates sustained attention rather than problem-solving/reasoning, so it does not address the review’s target task domain.

This is a task-based fMRI language-comprehension study about mood effects on discourse integration, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does report task-related activations (I1, I4) but the task domain does not match the meta-analysis objective.

Study involves depressed inpatients (no healthy adult sample) and uses a moral valence decision task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task—does not meet the review population or task requirements.

This is a PET study (not task-based fMRI) focused on perceptual anticipation of movement rather than problem-solving, so it fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem-solving tasks in the review objective.

The study used PET (not fMRI), so it is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study and meets exclusion criterion E4. The task also targets syntactic sentence comprehension rather than problem-solving, but exclusion is clear based on imaging modality.

The study used task-based fMRI in adults for language-processing tasks, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (I2 not met). Other inclusion criteria (I1, I3, I4) appear satisfied, but the primary task domain does not match the meta-analysis objective.

This study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement (E4). The task is working memory (2-back), not clearly a problem-solving task, but the imaging modality exclusion is definitive.
This is an fMRI study of reward processing (hypothetical monetary rewards) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore does not meet the review’s required task domain.

The study uses a color-word Stroop task assessing executive control (not a problem-solving or reasoning task) and focuses on intrinsic temporally coherent networks/predictive ongoing activity (connectivity emphasis) rather than task-based problem-solving activations relevant to the review.

Abstract mentions brain imaging and self-related cognition in major depressive episode but does not state it is a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, nor whether healthy adult participants were included. Insufficient information to determine eligibility from abstract alone; include for full-text review.

Abstract describes an fMRI MVPA methodological study but does not specify that participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task or that the sample included healthy adults. Insufficient information to determine whether I2 and I3 are met, so include for full-text review.

The fMRI task is a simple sustained attention task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting activation (and includes healthy controls), it does not examine problem solving in healthy adults.

Study used awake macaque monkeys (non-human primates). The review requires task-based fMRI in healthy adult humans (18–65); therefore the sample does not meet inclusion. Although it is a task-based fMRI study, the non-human sample clearly excludes it.

Methodological paper using simulated fMRI and diffusion-weighted imaging; abstract does not report empirical task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing problem-solving/reasoning or task-related activation results. Does not meet inclusion for healthy adult task-based problem solving.

Abstract describes a methodological/statistical test applied to regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) data comparing hypertensive and normotensive participants; it does not state task-based fMRI and rCBF typically refers to PET/SPECT rather than fMRI. Thus this is a non-fMRI study and should be excluded.

The study used passive story listening focused on semantic processing of motion verbs, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning). Other inclusion criteria (task-based fMRI, healthy adults, and reported task-related activation) appear met.

The study involves only Parkinson's disease patients (no healthy adult sample) and uses a gait/dual-task paradigm rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. It therefore fails the requirement for a healthy adult sample and does not address problem-solving in healthy adults.

Simultaneous EEG-fMRI during a mental calculation task indicates task-based fMRI with reported task-related BOLD activations and connectivity. Abstract does not specify participant age/health status; full text needed to confirm inclusion of healthy adults (18-65).

Study used task-based fMRI in children (ADHD and child controls) on a working memory task; it does not include healthy adults (18-65) and the task is not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task. Exclude due to no healthy adult sample.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation (I1, I4), the task involves viewing abstract paintings to assess implied motion rather than any problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet I2 (problem-solving), so it is excluded from the review.

This is a PET study ([H215O] PET), not a task-based fMRI study, and it examines recognition memory for words and faces rather than problem-solving/reasoning. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement (E4).

The study investigates auditory perceptual awareness (tone detection under informational masking), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it uses task-based fMRI and reports activation, it does not address the review objective of problem solving in healthy adults.

This is a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related BOLD responses during a two-digit number comparison task in adult men and women. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly label the task as 'problem-solving'; include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

The abstract reports an event-related task-based fMRI semantic decision experiment and task-related BOLD activation, satisfying I1 and I4. It is unclear whether the semantic decision qualifies as a ‘problem-solving or reasoning’ task (I2) and participant ages/health are not specified (I3). Due to this uncertainty, include for full-text review.

This is a theoretical/methodological study of fMRI effective connectivity (SEM vs partial correlations), not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study. It focuses on connectivity methods rather than task activation in healthy adults.

Study examines social interaction (live eye contact) and default-mode network, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI reporting activations (I1, I4) but not relevant to the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations/deactivations, but it does not specify that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor the participant age/health status. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3; recommend full-text review.

The study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations, but the task is passive narrative comprehension, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). Therefore it does not address the review objective.

This is a conceptual/review-style paper on epileptic networks (mentions reviewing evidence and implications) focusing on epilepsy and BOLD mapping of spikes/seizures, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Does not include healthy adult problem-solving tasks.

This study examines changes in resting-state functional connectivity after an fMRI neurofeedback activation epoch, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. The focus is on resting-state connectivity restructuring, so it meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.

The study reports fMRI in a single patient with an arteriovenous malformation performing a language (word-list generation) task; it does not include healthy adult participants nor a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The task is the Stroop Color-Word interference task (cognitive control/inhibition), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations, but does not address problem solving.

The study used PET imaging rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement (meets exclusion E4). Additionally, the task is semantic/letter judgment rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task relevant to the review.

Participants are children (no healthy adult sample) and the tasks involve reading aloud/naming rather than problem-solving or reasoning. Although task-based fMRI was used, the study does not include healthy adults (18–65) nor a problem-solving task, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract describes an event-related fMRI inspection-time task in non-demented older adults (age ~70). The fMRI task assesses processing speed, not problem-solving/reasoning, and the sample is older than the 18–65 target. Thus it fails the review's inclusion criteria (I2 and I3).

This fMRI study investigates visual motion imagery, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Although it is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation (I1, I4), it does not meet the review’s required task domain (I2). No listed exclusion criteria (E1–E4) are otherwise met.

Task-based fMRI in human participants reporting task-related BOLD activation and voxel patterns. Abstract does not specify that the tasks are explicitly problem-solving or reasoning, nor participant ages/health status, so include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a task-based fMRI study of sentence comprehension/prediction (language processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. The abstract also does not specify participant demographics, so inclusion of healthy adults cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

The task is language comprehension (reading/listening to a story), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), but it does not address problem solving.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation (left prefrontal) during semantic judgments. The abstract does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving or reasoning task as defined by the review, nor does it explicitly report participant ages; therefore inclusion for full-text review is warranted to determine eligibility.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of mental imagery vividness, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning). It does report task-related activation in sensory cortices and uses fMRI, but the task is imagery generation rather than problem solving.

This study reports EEG/ICA analysis of motor imagery and source localization using anatomical MRI; it is not a task-based fMRI study nor does it investigate problem-solving. Although fMRI BOLD foci are mentioned, no task-based fMRI results are reported. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion for non-fMRI studies.

The study uses task-based fMRI of rapid auditory stimulation to probe sensory LTP, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving). It does involve fMRI in adult humans and reports hemodynamic responses.

The study is an fMRI methods paper on overt picture naming and speech-related variance, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task). It is task-based (I1) but does not report task-related problem-solving activations.

The task involves anticipation of monetary rewards/punishments, not problem-solving or reasoning; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task). Abstract otherwise reports task-based fMRI in healthy adults.
The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of memory retrieval/false memories, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore does not meet the review’s essential inclusion criterion I2.

This is an animal (conscious rat) resting-state fMRI connectivity study, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans. It therefore fails the review’s required human healthy adult and task-based criteria.

This study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it fails the modality inclusion criterion. Although it involves a task and likely healthy control adults, the imaging technique excludes it from an fMRI-focused meta-analysis.
This is a PET study (not fMRI) investigating auditory-triggered mental imagery of object shape rather than problem-solving or reasoning; it therefore fails the fMRI requirement and is excluded (E4).
Study used PET (not fMRI) and investigated thirst/physiological sensation rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and the task-domain requirement.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI. The review requires fMRI studies of problem solving in healthy adults; therefore this non-fMRI imaging study meets exclusion criterion E4.
This is a PET study of motor sequence learning and motor imagery (foot movements), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving or reasoning. Excluded due to non-fMRI modality.

The study investigates complex syntactic (language) processing, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet I2. Though it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports activation/connectivity, its focus on connectivity/DM C and language processing makes it irrelevant to the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of visuospatial working memory but does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor whether a healthy adult (18–65) control group is included. Insufficient information to determine eligibility from abstract; include for full-text review.

Methodological fMRI time-series analysis using simulations; no human participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task and no task-related activation results reported. Does not meet inclusion criteria for task-based studies of problem solving in healthy adults.

The study uses task-based fMRI of trigeminal nociceptive (pain) stimulation in healthy volunteers, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task), despite meeting other fMRI and healthy adult criteria.

The abstract states this article is a review of fMRI methods, challenges, and future, not an original task-based fMRI study involving participants performing problem-solving tasks. It meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

This fMRI study investigates brain responses to acupuncture, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails the required inclusion criterion I2 (no problem-solving task), so it is not relevant to the review.
Study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the requirement for fMRI studies (E4).

Study examines interregional connectivity related to interictal spikes in temporal lobe epilepsy patients using partial-correlation of fMRI data. It is not a task-based problem-solving/reasoning study and involves patients rather than healthy adult participants.

The fMRI task is a simple sensory-motor task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2). Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy control participants and reports HRF results, it does not address the review objective.

This is a modeling study of oxygen delivery dynamics, not a task-based fMRI study with human participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task. No healthy adult sample or task-related fMRI activation results are reported.

This study used PET to measure regional cerebral blood flow in depressed patients and controls, not task-based fMRI. It therefore does not meet the requirement for fMRI methods assessing task-related problem solving activations.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of moyamoya disease patients (with healthy controls) and does not involve any task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a review article synthesizing fMRI studies of schizophrenia and antipsychotic effects (meets E1). It focuses on patient samples and does not report task-based problem-solving fMRI results in healthy adults (meets E3).

This is a computational/modeling study producing simulated EEG and fMRI BOLD signals; it does not include human participants or report task-based fMRI activation for a problem-solving task. Excluded due to no healthy adult sample and not an empirical fMRI task study.

The study uses a breath-hold physiological task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). Although it is a task-based fMRI study reporting BOLD results, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

Study enrolls participants with amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI), not healthy adults 18–65, so it does not include the required healthy adult sample. Additionally, tasks are working-memory rather than problem-solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

This paper is explicitly a review of fMRI in preclinical and early Alzheimer’s disease rather than a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report original task-related activation results for problem-solving in a healthy adult sample, meeting exclusion criterion E1.

The study examines BOLD hemodynamics in primary visual cortex during transient activations/deactivations, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving). It does report task-related BOLD responses and uses fMRI (I1, I4), but the population/task are not relevant to the review objective.

This is a resting-state EEG-fMRI study (eyes-open resting state) and does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.
Study used positron emission tomography (PET) during remifentanil infusion, not task-based fMRI of problem solving; thus it is a non-fMRI study and does not address the review objective.
This is a review article focusing on resting-state BOLD fMRI in hepatic encephalopathy (clinical population), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is an fMRI study of phobia-related word processing in spider-phobic and control subjects, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it includes healthy adult controls and reports task-related activations, it does not meet the review’s required task domain (problem solving).

Event-related task-based fMRI study of participants solving Chinese riddles; reports task-related activations and connectivity differences (insight vs noninsight). Abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state a healthy adult sample, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

Study sample consists of men with obstructive sleep apnea (clinical population), not healthy adults; tasks are working memory and finger tapping rather than problem-solving or reasoning. Although task-based BOLD-fMRI and activation results are reported, the study does not include a healthy adult sample and does not address problem-solving, so it is excluded.

Study uses PET (H2 15O) rather than fMRI and examines sexual arousal/penile erection, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and does not address the review topic.

Participants are adolescents (not healthy adults 18–65) and the task (go/no-go) is not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus the study does not include the required healthy adult sample for the review.

This study used PET (not fMRI) and investigated persistent symptoms after mild head trauma (clinical post-concussion sample), not task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Does not meet fMRI modality or healthy adult criteria.

The study uses PET ([15O]-butanol) rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the requirement for task-based fMRI studies. The abstract also does not mention problem-solving tasks. Excluded based on E4 (Non-fMRI studies).
This is a PET study of audiovisual speech perception (not task-based fMRI) and does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it fails the imaging modality criterion (non-fMRI).

This is an auditory task-based fMRI study examining venous contributions to BOLD in the auditory cortex; participants performed auditory stimulation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow (not fMRI), and it examines memory tasks in older women (55+), not problem-solving/reasoning in a healthy adult fMRI sample. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement.
The study used PET imaging (positron emission tomography), not task-based fMRI. The meta-analysis focuses on task-based fMRI studies, so this is a non-fMRI study and must be excluded.
This is a review article focused on technical aspects of fMRI in awake rats (preclinical), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adult humans.

This is a connectivity/methods-focused fMRI study using Granger causality and LASSO to analyze directed interregional interactions. It does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks nor report task-related activation for such tasks. It is a connectivity-only study, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

The study uses task-based BOLD-fMRI to investigate infantile esotropia in infants/children with a clinical condition, not healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks; therefore it does not meet the population or task criteria for this review.

Non-fMRI PET study measuring resting rCBF in a single Parkinson's disease patient undergoing PPN-DBS; not a task-based problem-solving study and includes no healthy adult participants. Excluded on multiple grounds.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI paradigm (symptom provocation) and task-related BOLD activation/variability results, satisfying I1 and I4. It does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task or clearly state inclusion of healthy adult participants, so full-text review is needed to determine I2 and I3.

The study used post-mortem subjects and therefore does not include healthy adult participants. It also investigates imaging artifacts from electrical stimulation rather than task-based problem-solving activation, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study of ankle dorsiflexion (a motor control task) in healthy young adults that reports BOLD activations. However, it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This is a PET study (not fMRI) investigating visual perception/apparent motion rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks in the context of the review. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem solving.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting BOLD responses during a working memory task (task-related activation). However, the review targets problem-solving/reasoning tasks and the abstract does not clarify that the working memory task meets that criterion. Insufficient information to assess I2 — include for full-text review.

The task is appetitive conditioning/reward learning, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adult controls and reports activations (meets I1, I3, I4) but fails the core topic requirement for inclusion.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study of visual cortex; it does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

Study used PET (positron emission tomography), not task-based fMRI, so it fails the imaging modality requirement for this review. Additionally the task is a memory retrieval task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Healthy controls are present, but the non-fMRI modality clearly excludes inclusion.

The study reports task-based fMRI of emotional processing in patients with generalized anxiety disorder and does not mention healthy adult controls or a problem-solving/reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult sample performing problem-solving. Exclusion criterion E3 applies.

The study investigates children with ADHD using task-based fMRI; there is no healthy adult (18-65) sample. The task addresses attentional networks rather than problem-solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

The study examines psilocybin effects on spontaneous/resting-state brain dynamics and connectivity, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI paradigm. It focuses on resting-state/connectivity measures, so it does not meet the review objective.
The study investigates EEG and BOLD responses to repeated visual stimuli (sensory/visual stimulation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

The fMRI task is a memory recognition (previously viewed vs. novel faces), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2. The study does meet I1, I3, and I4 (task-based fMRI, healthy middle-aged adults, reports task-related activations).

This study used PET ([15O]H2O) rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement (E4). The task is simulated driving, not a problem-solving/reasoning task, but the primary clear exclusion is non-fMRI imaging modality.

Abstract describes a review/introduction to fMRI methods and exemplary studies in psychiatry and psychotherapy rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI experiment of problem solving in healthy adults. This meets the exclusion criterion for reviews (E1).

The study uses task-based fMRI of passive natural movie viewing and develops encoding/decoding models; it does not involve participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). Therefore it is not relevant to the review objective.

Task is visual stimulation, not a problem-solving/reasoning task (fails I2). Although it is task-based fMRI and includes healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4), it does not address the review objective of problem solving.

Abstract reports fMRI investigation of chronic daily headache patients and aims to study pathophysiology during headache bouts. It does not describe a problem-solving or reasoning task nor a healthy adult sample; participants appear clinical. Therefore it fails the healthy adult sample requirement (E3).

The study uses an ‘eyes’ theory-of-mind task (social cognition), not a problem-solving/reasoning task required by the review. Participants are offspring from multiplex alcohol-dependence families (not a general healthy adult sample). Therefore it does not meet the review’s population and task requirements.

Single-case EEG/fMRI study of a 17-year-old patient with epilepsy; no healthy adult participants (no sample of healthy adults aged 18–65) and not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study. Thus it does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria.
The study uses PET (non-fMRI) imaging and focuses on Alzheimer’s disease imaging rather than task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults; it does not meet the review's fMRI requirement.

The study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of spontaneous mind-wandering/low-frequency BOLD connectivity, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning study. It therefore meets the resting-state/connectivity exclusion.

Study is an overview of fMRI in zebra finches (non-human animal model) and not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans; it appears to be a review/technique overview. Exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E1 (review) apply.

This is a PET study in patients with social anxiety disorder performing an anxiogenic public-speaking task, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and does not include a healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI study of an abstract symbol-manipulation (problem-solving) task in human participants; reports task-related BOLD activation and involves (presumably) healthy adult participants. Matches inclusion criteria I1, I2, I3, and I4 based on the abstract.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study using sliding-window PCA to examine dynamic FC; it is connectivity-focused and not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Exclusion based on resting-state/connectivity focus.

The study uses task-based fMRI of responses to food cues in obese participants rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, and the sample comprises obese (not healthy) adults. It therefore fails the healthy adult criterion.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants and task-related activations, but the task is prospective memory rather than an explicit problem-solving/reasoning task. Unclear if it fits the review's problem-solving definition, so include for full-text review.

Study used PET (not fMRI) and examined patients with chronic renal failure; it does not report task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and lacks a clear healthy adult sample.
This is a PET imaging study (not fMRI). The review requires task-based fMRI; therefore the study is a non-fMRI study and must be excluded.
Participants are adolescents aged 10–16; the study includes no adult (18–65) sample and therefore fails the requirement for a healthy adult group (I3).

Participants are treatment-seeking smokers (clinical sample), not a healthy adult sample, and the task is an N-back working memory task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the review requirement for healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Exclusion criterion E3 applies.

This study used PET (rCBF measured with H2(15)O), not task-based fMRI, which directly violates exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI studies). Although participants are healthy adults and task-related activation is reported, the imaging modality excludes it from the fMRI-focused review.

Abstract describes a resting-state functional connectivity fMRI study in dementia/Alzheimer's, not a task-based fMRI problem-solving study in healthy adults; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.
This is a PET study (15O-butanol), not an fMRI study, and it investigates visual motion aftereffects rather than problem-solving tasks in adults. Therefore it fails the fMRI inclusion requirement.

Task-based fMRI study with human participants performing inductive reasoning/problem-solving tasks; reports task-related BOLD activation. Abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health, so full-text review is needed to confirm healthy adult sample (include for full-text screening).

This study is a PET perfusion imaging study (not fMRI) and appears to be resting/clinical imaging in Prader-Willi patients rather than a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review requirements.
Study uses an emotional face-threat task (amygdala response), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet the review’s task requirement (I2).

Study uses healthy adolescents (not adults 18-65) and examines impulsivity/inhibition (Stop Signal Task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although task-based fMRI and activations are reported, population and task criteria are not met.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation during a working memory task in cognitively normal middle-aged adults (ages 40–60). It meets I1, I3, and I4. Unclear whether the working memory task qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2), so include for full-text review to resolve this uncertainty.

This study used PET (histamine H1 receptor occupancy and rCBF), not task-based fMRI, and the tasks were attention/visual discrimination rather than problem-solving/reasoning. It therefore fails the modality requirement. Exclude based on E4 (Non-fMRI study).

The study uses an event-related motor task rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does satisfy I1 and I3 (task-based fMRI and healthy adult participants).
This is a methodological/overview report (NMR Task Group) on BOLD fMRI principles and applications, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving; thus it's a review.

This is a cardiac imaging study using BOLD CMR and PET to assess myocardial oxygenation and perfusion, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve brain fMRI or cognitive/problem-solving tasks.

The task involves face perception/adaptation, not problem-solving or reasoning, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), but the content is outside the review scope.

This is a review article summarizing advances in neuroimaging (title and abstract indicate overview of PET/fMRI/SPECT) and does not report original task-based fMRI experiments or task-related activation results in healthy adults, so it meets the review exclusion criterion.

This is a resting-state PET (rCBF) longitudinal study, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. It does not involve task-related fMRI activation in healthy adults. Excluded based on non-fMRI imaging modality and resting-state design.

Abstract describes rCBF and depressive symptoms in older adults, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy 18–65 adults. Methods focus on regional cerebral blood flow (not fMRI) and sample are older adults with depressive symptoms.
Abstract reports fMRI BOLD responses to visual stimuli and memory span changes after creatine supplementation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
Study uses fMRI of face viewing (social/racial perception), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
exclusion_criteria_applied: Does not assess problem-solving/reasoning (I2 not met)

screening_results.reason: The study uses a visuo-motor tracking task to assess effects of cannabis on driving-related psychomotor and brain activity, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet the required I2 criterion for problem-solving, so it is excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study of sadness in children (not adults) and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore lacks a healthy adult sample and is outside the review scope.

The study used a visual attention task during sleep deprivation rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activation (I1, I4), but the task domain is outside the review objective.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI applications including a mental rotation task (a problem-solving/spatial reasoning paradigm) and reports differentiation of activation patterns; however participant demographics (healthy adults) are not specified. Include for full-text review to confirm samples and detailed activation results.

The abstract describes a task-based event-related fMRI study in healthy subjects (n=10) reporting task-related brain activations. It does not clearly state that the angle-discrimination task constitutes problem-solving/reasoning nor provide participant ages; include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

The fMRI task is motor sequence learning (explicit sequence learning), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). Abstract otherwise reports task-based fMRI in healthy adults (I1, I3, I4 satisfied) but does not assess problem solving.

This is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult controls reporting task-related BOLD results, but the task assesses empathy (cartoon-based empathy subcomponents), not problem solving or reasoning. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract uses fMRI and reports recovery of task-induced neural activation, but it does not specify that the task was a problem-solving or reasoning task nor provide participant age/health details. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

This fMRI study examines dopaminergic modulation of reward anticipation (monetary reward/loss), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It includes healthy adults and reports task activations, but fails to meet the review’s key inclusion criterion of a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2).

The study investigates visual ocular-dominance stimulation and decoding of BOLD timing differences, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving). It is task-based fMRI but irrelevant to the review objective.

Abstract lacks sufficient information to confirm task-based fMRI, a problem-solving/reasoning task, or inclusion of healthy adults aged 18–65. The study appears to involve pediatric brain tumor survivors (likely not a healthy adult sample). Full-text needed to determine eligibility.

This is a resting-state fMRI study examining spontaneous BOLD fluctuations before/after food intake, not a task-based problem-solving paradigm; it does not report task-related activations. Exclusion per E2 (resting-state studies).

The study uses task-based fMRI of sentence comprehension in patients with FTD and 11 healthy seniors. The healthy sample are described as 'seniors' (not 18–65), so it does not include the required healthy adult (18–65) group. Additionally the task is language comprehension, not problem-solving. Exclusion based on no eligible healthy adult sample.

The study uses PET (H2(15)O) to measure cerebral blood flow, not task-based fMRI and does not involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. It is therefore a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review focus.

Abstract describes fMRI effects of methylphenidate in adult men with ADHD and does not mention any healthy control or healthy adult sample. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample) and is not eligible for this review of healthy adults.

Abstract does not describe a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It summarizes PET, SPECT, and MRI findings in cirrhotic patients (with comparisons to controls) and reports metabolic/flow changes, not task-related fMRI activation. Excluded under E4 (non-fMRI studies).

Abstract describes longitudinal assessment of regional cerebral blood flow in older hypertensive and healthy older adults; no mention of fMRI, task-based design, or problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Participants are described as older adults (likely >65), not the 18–65 healthy adult sample required. Thus it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activations during tactile maze navigation (a problem-solving/spatial navigation task) in human participants. Age range not specified in abstract, so include for full-text confirmation of adult sample.

Study is an animal (rodent) pharmacological MRI model; no human participants or healthy adult sample and it does not involve human problem-solving tasks. Does not meet inclusion for human fMRI problem-solving studies.
The study uses PET to measure rCBF (not fMRI) and investigates semantic/episodic memory retrieval, not problem-solving tasks in fMRI. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement for the review.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (Broca’s and auditory areas), but the task is a language task—not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task—and participant age/health status are not reported. Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the problem-solving criterion and whether a healthy adult sample (18–65) was used.

This is an fMRI study of visuospatial angle discrimination in mild Alzheimer’s patients with age-matched controls. The task is perceptual discrimination, not a problem-solving/reasoning task, and the sample comprises clinical/likely older adults rather than healthy adults aged 18–65. Thus it does not meet the review's inclusion criteria.
