Study examines neural responses to sadness in early life and genetic influences, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. Abstract implies an early-life (non-adult) sample rather than healthy adults (18-65), so it does not meet the review population criterion.

This article is a review of spontaneous BOLD fluctuations and intrinsic functional architecture (resting-state/connectivity) rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (exclusion E1) and focuses on resting-state/connectivity (exclusion E2).

This is an explicit review article of EEG-fMRI studies on absence seizures in patients, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not include a healthy adult task-based sample.

The abstract describes seizure network activity in patients and animal models, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report problem-solving or reasoning tasks nor a healthy adult (18–65) sample. Exclusion on E3 is clear.

This is a review of fMRI studies in prematurely born infants/children, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It lacks an adult healthy sample and appears to be a review article.

This study used non-human (gerbil) subjects and investigated vibrissal stimulation in barrel cortex, so it does not include healthy adult human participants or any problem-solving task. Therefore it fails the human healthy adult sample requirement and is outside the review scope.

This study examines BOLD signal differences in visual (magnocellular/parvocellular) pathways in primary visual cortex using visual stimuli; it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). Although it is task-based fMRI and reports task-related BOLD time courses, it is not relevant to the meta-analysis objective.

This is a PET study using H2(15)O and covariance-based functional connectivity analysis of the Stroop task — not task-based fMRI nor a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E2 (connectivity-only study).

This study uses a visual checkerboard stimulus to localize VEP and fMRI sources and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). It is task-based, includes healthy adults, and reports task-related activation.

This is a resting-state fMRI study comparing chronic tinnitus patients and healthy controls; no task-based paradigm or problem-solving/reasoning task is reported. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state studies and does not address the review objective.

This study uses emotion/authenticity rating tasks (emotion recognition) rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Although it is task-based fMRI reporting BOLD effects, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults and thus does not meet the review objective.

This is an animal (rat) study of noxious forepaw stimulation, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study in human healthy adults. It therefore does not include a healthy adult human sample and does not address problem-solving tasks.

This is a task-based fMRI study of facial affect recognition in individuals with high-functioning autism; no healthy adult (18-65) sample is reported and the task is not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Excluded due to absence of a healthy adult sample.

The abstract explicitly states this is a review of BOLD fMRI literature rather than an original task-based fMRI experiment. It does not report primary data on problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

Abstract describes an fMRI methodological technique and does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task or confirm healthy adult participants. Insufficient information to determine eligibility for a review of task-based problem-solving in healthy adults; include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study of inductive reasoning in human participants; reports task-related BOLD activation and connectivity. Abstract does not specify ages of the ‘normal aging’ group, so inclusion criterion I3 (healthy adults 18–65) cannot be confirmed from the abstract — include for full-text review to determine age range.

This is explicitly a review article about postlesional language plasticity in patients (stroke, epilepsy, brain tumors) rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Thus it meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The article is a review/chapter summarizing task-based fMRI studies (not original research). It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review or meta-analysis). Insufficient as an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults.

This is a review article focused on nuclear medicine (SPECT, PET, MRS) in stroke recovery, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI review, so it does not meet the review's inclusion criteria.

Study used a working memory task with emotional distracters in remitted depressed patients—not a problem-solving/reasoning task relevant to the review, and abstract reports patient sample without mention of a healthy adult group. Does not meet the review objective.

This article is a narrative review of intrinsic brain activity and clinical applications, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activation in healthy adult problem-solving. Meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

The study is a validation of SPECT/PET autoradiography for cerebrovascular reactivity; it is not a task-based fMRI study nor does it involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Excluded because it is a non-fMRI imaging study (E4).

This is a methodological fMRI/QSM methods paper; the abstract does not describe any problem-solving or reasoning task, does not report task-related activation for problem solving, and does not clearly state a healthy adult sample. It therefore does not meet the review objective.

Animal fMRI study in alpha-chloralose–anesthetized rats using forepaw stimulation; no human participants or healthy adult sample and not a problem-solving task. Not relevant to task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD responses in young adults, but the task is face novelty detection, not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task. Include for full-text review to confirm task type and sample health status.

Abstract reports an event-related task-based fMRI study examining dorsolateral PFC activation and task-related activity in schizophrenia. It clearly meets I1 and I4. However, the abstract does not state whether a healthy adult control group was included (I3) nor that the task was a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2). Insufficient information to determine inclusion — include for full-text review.
Study uses visual checkerboard stimulation to examine neural/VEP–BOLD coupling, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults; it does not address the review objective.

The study is a resting-state/connectivity and computational modeling study (not task-based problem-solving). Participants are adolescents with early-onset schizophrenia and adolescent controls, not healthy adults aged 18-65.

This study used a task-free (resting-state/pharmacological) fMRI protocol with psilocybin and did not involve participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the task-based/problem-solving inclusion criterion.

This is a narrative review of neuroimaging in fibromyalgia, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It summarizes studies of pain sensitivity, cognitive dysfunction, and various neuroimaging techniques in clinical samples; thus it meets the exclusion criterion for reviews.

The study used photic visual stimulation to combine DTI and fMRI, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). It is task-based fMRI and reports activation, but does not address the review objective of problem solving.

The study uses task-based fMRI of auditory and somatosensory stimulation in a patient with a thalamic lesion and controls, examining synesthesia. It does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not address the review objective.

This study uses tDCS with pseudo-continuous arterial spin labeling during a psychosocial stress task (TSST), not task-based BOLD fMRI, and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI/problem-solving requirements.

The study investigates speech motor control, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (does not meet I2). The abstract does not indicate a healthy adult problem-solving paradigm, so it is not relevant to the meta-analysis objective.

This is an animal (rat) study using optical imaging to measure microvascular O2 and flow, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults performing problem-solving tasks. It does not include healthy adult humans or fMRI methods.

This is a perfusion MRI study in patients with early HIV-cognitive motor complex, not a task-based fMRI study and it does not include healthy adult participants. Excluded for being non-fMRI and lacking healthy adult sample.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study of equation solving with task-related BOLD responses in specific brain regions, meeting I1, I2, and I4. The abstract does not explicitly state participant age/health status (I3), so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult sample.

This study used H2(15)O PET (with TMS) rather than task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI modality requirement. The reported task (Stroop) is not a problem-solving/reasoning task, but the primary exclusion is non-fMRI.

The study examines BOLD responses to median-nerve somatosensory stimulation in acute ischemic stroke patients (clinical sample) and does not involve problem-solving tasks or include a healthy adult group. Excluded because there is no healthy adult sample (E3).

The study used PET (not fMRI) and reports longitudinal rCBF correlations in elderly men rather than task-based fMRI. Participants are older adults (elderly), not the target healthy adult 18–65 group, and it does not examine task-based problem-solving fMRI activation.

The task is motor imagery of coordination exercises, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2 despite being a task-based fMRI study of healthy adults reporting activation.
Resting-state connectivity study in a single 6-year-old imaged pre/post callosotomy; not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.
This is a PET study measuring regional cerebral blood flow using [(15)O]-butanol, not a task-based fMRI study. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement for the review.

This is a task-based fMRI study in humans that includes healthy control subjects and reports task-related BOLD activation. However, the task (speaking about Rorschach inkblots) does not clearly constitute a problem-solving or reasoning task per the review objective. Insufficient information to confirm I2 from the abstract alone; include for full-text review.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI using financial tasks but does not clearly state that tasks constitute problem-solving/reasoning, nor explicitly report participant characteristics or detailed task-related activation results. Insufficient information to determine I2–I4, so include for full-text review.

The study reports physiological calibration using respiratory-calibrated MRI and fNIRS in healthy adults and does not describe any task-based paradigm or problem-solving/reasoning task, nor task-related activation results; it is therefore not relevant to the review.

Participants are children/adolescents (ages 3–18); no healthy adult (18–65) sample and the task is narrative comprehension rather than problem-solving. Therefore it does not meet the adult problem-solving task inclusion criteria.

The study used visual stimulation (intermittent photic stimulation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults reporting activation, but the task is unrelated to problem solving.

Abstract reports task-based brain activity (fMRI) in response to problem-solving/working-memory stimulation and likely presents task-related activation results. However, it does not specify whether a healthy adult (18–65) control/sample is included. Insufficient info to rule out E3, so include for full-text review.

This study used PET (15O-water) rather than task-based fMRI, so it fails the imaging modality requirement (I1). Although it includes healthy adults and reports task-related activation, the non-fMRI method excludes it from this review.

This is a task-based fMRI study of smooth pursuit eye movements assessing perceptual stability, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). It does report task-related activation (I1, I4) but does not involve problem solving in healthy adults.

This is an animal (isoflurane-anesthetized cats) fMRI study using visual temporal-frequency stimuli, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning study in human adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult human sample or relevant task.

This study reports altered functional connectivity in patients with REM sleep behavior disorder (connectivity-only), and involves a patient sample with no indication of a healthy adult group. It is not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study. Exclusion criteria E2 and E3 apply.

This is a PET study of vowel production and functional connectivity, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the meta-analysis objective (problem-solving fMRI).

This is a PET study (not fMRI) and involves Parkinson’s disease patients with deep brain stimulation rather than healthy adult participants; thus it fails the modality and population inclusion criteria.

Study examines longitudinal regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF) in an older adult (BLSA) sample rather than task-based fMRI, and the sample consists of older adults (aging sample likely >65), not healthy adults aged 18–65. Thus it fails the review's I1 and I3 requirements.
Study sample consists of typically developing adolescents; no healthy adult (18–65) group is reported. Meta-analysis requires inclusion of healthy adults, so exclude based on participant age.

This is a methods paper about Kalman-filter-based reconstruction of stimulus functions in fMRI, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving. The abstract does not report healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving task or task-related activation results.

This is a narrative review of quantitative fMRI and brain energy metabolism, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. The abstract describes synthesis of methods and findings rather than reporting task-related activation in a healthy adult sample.

This is a review article focused on resting-state fMRI and connectivity, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

Abstract describes MRI methods to recover susceptibility-induced signal loss and does not mention a task-based problem-solving paradigm, healthy adult participants, or task-related activation. Because no explicit exclusion criterion is met but inclusion criteria are not confirmed from the abstract, include for full-text review to determine relevance.

This article is a methodological/technical review of DTI and fMRI rather than an original task-based fMRI study. It does not report participant samples or task-related activation for problem-solving in healthy adults.

Study reports EEG-fMRI results from a single patient with fixation-off sensitivity (clinical population), not healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Does not address task-based problem solving in healthy adults.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activations, the task is a visuomotor/joystick paradigm studying coordinate transformations for goal-directed hand movements — not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).
Participants are children/adolescents (ages 9–19); although task-based fMRI (Stroop) was used, the study does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample required by the review.

This is a methodological/review article discussing fMRI methods and interpretation, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Abstract contains no primary participants, tasks, or task-related activation results.

Abstract reports task-related brain activation during a working memory task in adult participants but does not explicitly state that imaging was fMRI nor that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task. Insufficient information to confirm I1 and I2, so include for full-text review.

Participants are children (including autism spectrum disorder) and typically developing children, not healthy adults 18–65. The task is passive face viewing ('self' vs 'other'), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the review population or task criteria.

The study uses fixation-based fMRI during free viewing of visual images and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails Inclusion Criterion I2. It does report fMRI task-related activations (I1, I4), but the core task is not problem solving.

This is an animal (dog) study using 99mTc-ECD SPECT to measure regional cerebral blood flow after morphine; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants or problem-solving task. Does not meet the review aims.

The study uses a working-memory task in nicotine-dependent smokers and does not address problem-solving/reasoning tasks. The abstract reports data from smokers (no healthy adult control group mentioned), so it does not meet the review’s population or task requirements.

This article is a review of BOLD/fMRI methods and clinical applications, not a primary task-based fMRI study with participants performing problem-solving. It contains no original healthy adult task activation results. Exclusion: review (E1).

This is a technical/methods BOLD-fMRI study investigating flip-angle selection and physiological noise, using a visual-motor task. It does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet the review objective (I2).

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in a sample of healthy adults and provides task-related activation results, but it is unclear whether the reversal learning task qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task. Include for full-text review to clarify I2.

This is a methods/connectivity study using multi-echo fMRI to separate BOLD and non-BOLD signals and improve seed-based functional connectivity; it does not report task-based problem-solving or reasoning in healthy adults. Excluded as a resting-state/connectivity-focused study.

Abstract describes a methodological investigation of localization in BOLD fMRI and simulations; it does not clearly report a task-based problem-solving or reasoning experiment in healthy adults. Information is insufficient to determine whether any included/analysed studies involved problem-solving tasks, so include for full-text review.

The study used PET to measure regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF), not task-based fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI modality requirement for the meta-analysis. Additionally, the tasks involve attention/arousal and learning rather than problem-solving, but the primary exclusion is non-fMRI methodology.

The study uses task-based fMRI to decode visual/perceptual states in a virtual combat environment (viewing characters), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not address the review objective (fails I2).
Task-based fMRI study of grapheme–colour synaesthesia and colour perception, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 for the review.

This is a PET study (not fMRI), and participants were clinical outpatients with anxiety disorders (no healthy adult sample). The task is symptom provocation, not problem-solving. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I1/I2/I3 and meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

The study used 99mTc-Bicisate SPECT (not fMRI) and examined inhibition and working memory in alcohol-dependent patients; it does not report task-based fMRI results for problem-solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation components, but it does not specify that participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task or explicitly state a healthy adult sample. Insufficient information to determine inclusion for the review, so include for full-text screening.

Task-based fMRI study using the Tower of London planning task (problem-solving), reports task-related BOLD activation (left SPC), and involves healthy adult participants. Abstract provides clear task activation results relevant to problem-solving.

The study reports task-based fMRI with task-related striatal BOLD results (reversal-learning task). However, it is unclear whether reversal learning qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task and participant health status/ages are not explicitly stated. Include for full-text review to determine if the task meets the review's problem-solving definition and if participants are healthy adults.

The study used isoflurane-anesthetized cats (non-human animals) rather than healthy adult humans, so it does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample. It also does not investigate problem-solving tasks. Exclusion based on E3.

This study reports two case reports of sport-related concussion patients undergoing fMRI; it does not include a group of healthy adult participants (18–65) performing problem-solving/reasoning tasks, so it fails inclusion criterion I3.

Task involves face encoding/recognition in schizophrenia vs. controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adult controls (meets I1, I3, I4), it does not address the review objective.

The study uses breath-hold respiratory calibration, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports activation, it does not address problem solving (I2 not met).

Task-based fMRI memory study in amnestic MCI patients (pattern-separation/recognition), not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. Abstract does not indicate a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a PET study of neural activity during ejaculation/sexual stimulation, not an fMRI study of problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore fails the non-fMRI criterion and does not address task-based problem solving in healthy adults.
Participants are recently abstinent stimulant-dependent patients with no healthy adult (18-65) group reported; therefore the study does not include the required healthy adult sample for the review.

Abstract reports a methods paper (power/sample-size via non-central RFT) and an application to a small BOLD fMRI dataset, but does not specify a task-based problem-solving/reasoning paradigm, healthy adult sample, or task-related activation results. Information is insufficient to determine eligibility; include for full-text review.

This is a longitudinal PET study of regional cerebral blood flow in nondemented older adults, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore does not meet the fMRI task-based requirement.

Abstract reports brain activation patterns underlying working memory performance but does not specify task-based fMRI, a problem-solving/reasoning task, or presence of healthy adult participants. Insufficient information to determine eligibility from abstract alone—include for full-text review.

The study uses task-based fMRI for language (sentence listening) but focuses on effective and structural connectivity (GCM and DTPM) rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It does not examine a problem-solving task in healthy adults; it is connectivity-focused, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing a virtual radial arm maze assessing working memory and decision-making; authors report task-related BOLD discrimination in DLPFC and V1. The abstract does not explicitly label the task as 'problem-solving' but decision-making/maze performance may meet the review's scope; include for full-text review to confirm.
Animal fMRI study in isoflurane-anesthetized cats; no human participants and not a problem-solving task. Does not include healthy adult human sample.
Participants are dogs (non-human); no healthy adult human sample is included, so the study does not meet the population criterion for this review.

This is an animal (rat) fMRI study of olfactory responses using odor stimulation, not a task-based problem-solving study in healthy adult humans. It therefore does not meet the population or task criteria for inclusion.

Study examines task-related fMRI in patients with clinically isolated syndrome focused on attentional processing; abstract does not indicate a healthy adult sample and task is attention (not problem-solving). Therefore it does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult problem-solving fMRI study.

Study used PET (not fMRI), so it fails the 'non-fMRI studies' exclusion (E4). Additionally, the task is episodic memory (not problem-solving) and participants are MCI patients and elderly controls rather than healthy adults aged 18–65, making it irrelevant to the review.

The study tests effects of high-temporal-resolution fMRI on Granger causality connectivity estimates during a visuomotor task. It is a connectivity-focused analysis (Granger causality) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults, so it does not address the review objective. Exclusion criterion E2 (connectivity-only study) applies.

The task is a lexical-decision semantic-priming study of lexical ambiguity, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review objective. Although it is a task-based fMRI study reporting activations in healthy participants, it does not investigate problem solving.
Study involves PET and MRI in multiple sclerosis patients, not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. No healthy adult sample and not an fMRI task study of problem solving.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in humans including healthy adults and reports task-related BOLD activation. However, the task is spatial navigation/allocentric memory rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task, so inclusion criterion I2 is uncertain; include for full-text review to determine whether the task qualifies.

Task-based 7T fMRI study that reports task-related BOLD activations and manipulation of cognitive load. The abstract does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving or reasoning task nor provide participant age range, so include for full-text review to determine whether I2 and I3 are met.

This study uses a pharmacological fMRI challenge (CCK-4) and ICA to characterize neural networks; it does not involve participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although healthy adults were studied and fMRI activations are reported, the study is connectivity/pharmacology-focused rather than task-based problem solving, so it does not meet the review’s objective.

The study uses task-based fMRI of emotional face perception, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2. The abstract clearly reports fMRI activation and includes healthy young adults, so I1, I3, and I4 are met. Exclusion is based on lack of a problem-solving task rather than any listed exclusion criterion (E1–E4).

This is a task-based fMRI study using vibrotactile sensory stimulation in patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 and is not relevant to the meta-analysis.

Study used PET (not fMRI) and involved older adults from an aging cohort rather than healthy adults in the 18–65 range; additionally, tasks were memory recognition rather than problem-solving. These fit exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

The study used resting-state fMRI connectivity (DMN) and DTI in ALS patients and controls rather than task-based fMRI during a problem-solving or reasoning task. Thus it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

The study used a working memory (modified Sternberg) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). It is task-based fMRI and reports activation (I1, I4), but the task does not match the review objective of problem solving in healthy adults.

This is a task-based fMRI study of lower-limb stepping (motor task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task). Participants are healthy adults and task-related activation is reported, but the task is outside review scope.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of remitted geriatric depression (no task-based problem-solving), and participants are geriatric (older than 65), not healthy adults aged 18–65. Does not meet inclusion for task-based problem-solving in healthy adults.

The study examines stroke patients' motor function using DTI and task-based BOLD fMRI; it does not include a healthy adult sample nor involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks relevant to the review objective.

This fMRI study examined brain responses to glucose vs fructose infusions and did not involve any problem-solving or reasoning task. It is not a task-based cognitive fMRI study relevant to the review objective.

Study uses fNIRS (non-fMRI), examines resting-state functional connectivity in infants (not healthy adults), and focuses on connectivity rather than task-based problem solving; thus it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

Participants passively watched natural movie segments; the study investigates EEG/fMRI mapping of sensory responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not address the review objective (problem-solving in healthy adults).

This task-based fMRI study examines capsaicin-evoked urge-to-cough (sensory/irritation) and maps related brain activation. Participants are not performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it fails inclusion criterion I2 despite meeting other methodological criteria.

The task is a visuospatial paired-associates memory encoding/retrieval fMRI task in MCI patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). It does include task-based fMRI and reports activations, but the task domain is memory, so not eligible.

This is a PET case report of a single patient with cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, not an fMRI study, and does not include healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving task. It therefore fails criteria for imaging modality and participant sample.

The abstract describes pharmacologic (drug-induced) BOLD signal measurement using a blood-pressure clamp, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study in healthy adults. It appears to be a task-free/pharmacologic fMRI methodological study, so it does not meet the review’s focus on task-based problem-solving fMRI.

The study investigates implicit motor sequence learning (perceptuomotor tracking), not problem-solving or reasoning. Although it is a task-based fMRI study of healthy middle-aged adults reporting task-related activation (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet the required inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This is a methodology paper on EEG–fMRI transfer functions using resting-state and visual experiments; it does not investigate task-based problem-solving or reasoning in healthy adults. It does not address the review objective of problem-solving tasks.

Participants are adolescents (mean age 15.2), so there is no healthy adult (18-65) sample. Additionally, the task is a simple motor hand-opening/closing task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task relevant to the review objective.

The study uses task-based fMRI of visual motion predictive coding, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem solving). It does report fMRI activation (I1, I4) but does not address the review objective.

The article is explicitly described as a review ('In this review...') summarizing prior model-based studies rather than reporting original task-based fMRI data, therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis). Abstract does not present original task activation results.
The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of speech motor control (syllable repetition), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This is a resting-state dynamic connectivity fMRI study of spontaneous BOLD fluctuations in older adults (age 56–89). It does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm and does not include a healthy adult group within the 18–65 age range, so it fails inclusion.

The study is a task-based fMRI of affective touch perception in healthy adults and reports activations, but it does not involve any problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). Therefore it does not address the review objective.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI analyses and reports task-related BOLD/neurovascular results, but it does not indicate participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task nor specify participant ages/health. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3 — include for full-text review.

This is a task-based fMRI study in (presumably) healthy adults reporting task-related activations (satisfies I1, I3, I4). However, the task is numerosity/perceptual classification rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to determine whether the task meets the review's definition of problem solving.
The study used task-based fMRI but participants were Parkinson's disease patients only (n=15) with no healthy adult sample reported; therefore it fails the requirement for healthy adults (I3).

Participants are cocaine-dependent individuals (no healthy adult sample), and the task is emotional stress imagery rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore the study does not meet the review’s population or task requirements.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study with healthy control participants and task-related activation results, but it does not specify that the task was a problem-solving or reasoning task. Insufficient information to determine I2 from abstract; include for full-text review.

This is a review/opinion article focusing on MR imaging methods and non-human primate studies rather than a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adult humans. It is a review (E1) and does not report data from a healthy adult human problem-solving sample (E3).

Primary data are from electrocorticography (invasive ECoG) in 19 patients rather than task-based fMRI in healthy adults; participants are patients (not a healthy adult sample). Although fMRI comparisons are mentioned, this is not an fMRI study of healthy adults.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-modulated BOLD and task-related functional connectivity during cognitive tasks (parity judgments, naming). Abstract does not clearly state tasks are ‘problem-solving/reasoning’ nor specify participant ages/health, so insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3; include for full-text review.

This fMRI task study examines the concealed information test (deception/recognition), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. The abstract clearly reports task-related BOLD responses (I1 and I4 met), but the study does not address problem solving in healthy adults as required by the review.

Abstract indicates a task-based fMRI study of semantic word generation in schizophrenia but does not clearly state whether healthy adult controls were included or whether the task qualifies as problem-solving/reasoning. Insufficient information to determine relevance to problem-solving in healthy adults; include for full-text review to confirm participant groups and task nature.

The study uses a perceptual discrimination task (spatial frequency of Gabors), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI and reports activation but does not address problem solving.

The study uses an auditory choice reaction task focusing on gamma-band EEG–fMRI coupling, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does use task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), but does not address problem solving.

This is a task-based fMRI study of melody perception, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. While I1 and I4 are satisfied, the study is not relevant to the review objective.
Study is an animal (rat) optogenetic/fMRI experiment, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Does not include healthy adult human participants.

Abstract describes an fMRI task (moving dots) and reports activation results, but does not indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task nor state the sample includes healthy adults. Insufficient information on I2 and I3; include for full-text review.

This paper is a systematic review (meta-analysis) of vascular conditions in neuroimaging studies, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; thus it meets exclusion criterion E1.

Abstract describes fMRI methods and sensory/motor tasks (visual-motor, auditory-motor-visual) focused on imaging the initial negative BOLD response, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Thus it fails the review’s required inclusion criterion I2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of declarative memory (hydrocortisone effects) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). It reports task-related activation (I4) and is task-based fMRI (I1), but does not address problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is an fMRI study of visuospatial perception in Alzheimer’s patients and elderly comparison subjects, not a problem-solving/reasoning task in healthy adults 18–65. The healthy sample are described as ‘elderly’ (outside target age range), so it does not meet the review’s population or task criteria.

This is a review article about imaging hypoxia in glioma patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and concerns patient tumors rather than healthy adult participants (E3).

Abstract reports task-based fMRI and task-related activation (I1, I4). However, the task is a simple visual two-choice reaction task (not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task) and the abstract emphasizes patients with schizophrenia without clearly stating inclusion of a healthy adult control group. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

Study uses PET and SPET (non-fMRI) methods and focuses on patients with mild dementia/mild cognitive impairment rather than healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, so it fails inclusion criteria I1, I2, and I3 and meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

This paper is a coordinate-based meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials in postmenopausal women examining hormone therapy effects, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement. The abstract investigates schizophrenia patients and does not report task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

Abstract does not specify task-based fMRI, a problem-solving/reasoning task, or inclusion of healthy adult participants; insufficient information to determine eligibility, so include for full-text review.

This is a PET imaging study (FDG and DTBZ PET), not task-based fMRI, and it examines subjects with mild dementia/MCI rather than healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.
The fMRI task involved viewing photographic nature stimuli (sky compositions), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not address the review objective (fails I2).

The study reports task-based fMRI of sexual/erectile dysfunction during visual erotic stimulation in (psycho)genic ED patients — not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It therefore does not meet the review’s focus on problem-solving tasks in healthy adults.

The study decodes perceived handwritten characters from fMRI BOLD responses (a perception/decoding task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. The abstract also does not specify participants’ ages/health. Exclusion applied for lack of a clearly reported healthy adult sample.

Participants are chronic aphasia patients (clinical sample) and the task involves auditory language processing after therapy rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task in healthy adults. Thus no healthy adult sample is included.

Study uses a biological motion perception task in schizophrenia and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails inclusion criterion I2). It is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations.

Participants are treatment-seeking smokers (clinical sample), not healthy adults (fails I3). The task is an N-back working memory task, not a problem-solving/reasoning task (fails I2). Therefore the study does not meet the review population/task criteria and should be excluded.

The sample consists solely of nicotine-deprived smokers (no healthy adult control group reported), so it fails inclusion criterion I3. The study is task-based fMRI and reports activation, but lacks a healthy adult sample.
This is a resting-state fMRI/ASL pharmacological connectivity study, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study. It therefore meets exclusion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only).

This is a review paper (Quarterly Reviews of Biophysics) summarizing MRS and fMRI biophysics rather than reporting a task-based fMRI experiment of problem-solving in healthy adults. No task-related activation results or healthy adult sample are presented.

Task-based fMRI study in humans assessing two numerical tasks (number comparison, number bisection) with BOLD activation reported. Participants include healthy adult groups (OC-users, men, naturally cycling women). The tasks constitute numerical reasoning/problem-solving relevant to the review objective.

Participants are older adults (elders) rather than healthy adults aged 18–65, and the tasks are semantic and episodic memory (not problem-solving/reasoning). Thus the study does not meet the review’s population and task requirements.

The study uses task-based fMRI of memory-guided saccades (spatial memory and eye movement selection), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. The abstract does report task-related activations (I1, I4).
The study reports fMRI in macaque monkeys (non-human primates) rather than human participants; therefore it does not include a healthy adult human sample and is outside the review population.

The study uses a task-based episodic memory paradigm examining hippocampal activation related to APOE genotype — not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

Although this is a task-based fMRI study using the Tower of London problem-solving task and includes healthy controls, the abstract reports task-related functional coupling (connectivity) results rather than regional activation maps. This appears to be a connectivity-focused analysis (connectivity-only), meeting exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract describes an fMRI methodological study of overt speech/reading, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It clearly uses task-based fMRI methods (I1) but does not provide sufficient information that participants performed a problem-solving task (I2), or that it reports task-related activation in healthy adults (I3,I4). Insufficient information to determine relevance to problem solving; include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study using an N-back working memory task in healthy women and reporting BOLD results. The abstract does not indicate a problem-solving or reasoning task (N-back is working memory), and participant ages are not specified. Because I2 (problem-solving/reasoning) and I3 (explicit adult age range) are unclear from the abstract, include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Study investigates vascular reactivity in cerebral amyloid angiopathy patients and does not report a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. The abstract indicates a patient-focused fMRI application, so it does not meet the review population/task requirements.
Study is a resting-state EEG–fMRI connectivity study examining alpha power correlations, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving or reasoning. Does not address task-related activation.

The study used PET (not fMRI), so it fails exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI). Additionally, the tasks described are perceptual/object recognition (color decision, categorization), not problem-solving/reasoning tasks relevant to the review.

Study sample mean age 75 indicates older adults and does not include a healthy adult group aged 18–65. Additionally, the task involves memory performance rather than problem-solving. Excluded based on E3 (no healthy adult sample in target age range).

Study focuses on spontaneous BOLD events and functional connectivity dynamics (resting-state/connectivity investigation) rather than task-based problem-solving in healthy adults; does not report task-related problem-solving activation. Exclusion based on connectivity/resting-state focus.

Task-based fMRI in (presumably) healthy adult participants, reporting task-related brain activations (satisfies I1, I3, I4). However, the task is an N-back working memory paradigm, not explicitly a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm whether N-back meets the review's problem-solving criterion.

This study is an activation likelihood estimation meta-analysis (review/meta-analysis) of fMRI studies of reward processing in obesity and addictions, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This is an introductory/review chapter on slow brain oscillations across sleep, rest, and task contexts rather than a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report original task-related fMRI activation results and appears to be a review, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.
This is a resting-state fMRI study examining effects of cranial electrotherapy stimulation; no task or problem-solving paradigm is reported, so it meets exclusion E2.

This is a PET cerebral blood flow study in patients with idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It does not assess problem-solving tasks or include a healthy adult sample.
Participants are described as 'presurgical' with implanted electrodes (ECoG), indicating a patient sample; no healthy adult group is reported in the abstract, so criterion I3 is not met.

This is a task-based fMRI study of emotion regulation (viewing/regulating emotional pictures), not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2). It includes healthy participants and reports task activations, but the task does not match the review objective.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of IBS vs healthy controls and does not report any task-based problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state studies and is not relevant to task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

The study uses a visual face-encoding (memory) task, not a problem-solving/reasoning task (fails I2). Participants are MCI, AD, and healthy elderly controls (older adults), so it does not include healthy adults aged 18–65 (fails I3). Therefore it is not eligible for the meta-analysis of problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

Study involves patients with extra- or intracranial artery stenosis (no healthy adult sample) and uses a simple motor task (fist closure), not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the review’s target population or task requirements.

This is a task-based BOLD fMRI study of Stroop (inhibition/selective attention) tasks, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. Although it meets I1 and I4, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults, so it is excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study where participants perform causality judgements (a form of reasoning/problem-solving). The study includes a healthy control group and reports task-related BOLD activation results and connectivity differences, meeting inclusion criteria.

The task is mental imagery for communication/command-following in severely brain-injured patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task for healthy adults. Thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

Study investigates aphasic patients before and after therapy performing an overt language task; no healthy adult sample and task is language (not problem-solving). Therefore it does not meet the review requirement for healthy adult problem-solving fMRI (E3).

Study is on adolescents (not adults 18–65) and examines neural responses to food advertising, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult sample nor the target task domain.

This is an fMRI fear-conditioning study, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task). It does include healthy adults and task-based fMRI with activation results (I1, I3, I4).

This study examined EEG-vigilance and BOLD signals during rest (resting-state analysis), not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not address task-related activation for problem solving.

The abstract investigates regional homogeneity of intrinsic brain activity (ReHo) comparing happy and unhappy individuals, indicating a resting-state fMRI approach. No task-based or problem-solving/reasoning paradigm is described, so it does not meet inclusion criteria I1/I2 and matches exclusion E2 (resting-state study).

Abstract reports functional connectivity alterations in late-onset depression patients, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults. It focuses on connectivity (resting/connectivity analysis) and involves a clinical sample, so it fails the review’s task-based and healthy adult criteria.

Study reports simultaneous fMRI and electrophysiology in macaque monkeys (non-human), and investigates sensory motion processing rather than a problem-solving task in human adults. Does not include healthy adult human participants and so fails the review inclusion criteria.

The study examines voluntary movement initiation and slow BOLD oscillations, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It focuses on connectivity/time-series analyses rather than task-based problem-solving fMRI, so it does not meet review scope.
Abstract explicitly states this is a review article rather than an original task-based fMRI study and thus does not report primary task-related activation data in healthy adults. Excluded per E1.

Abstract describes dyadic/social interaction and theoretical framework for dfMRI; it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. Not relevant to review objective (task-based fMRI of problem solving).

The study investigates social impression formation (outcome-dependency effects) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), but the task is not problem solving, so excluded from this review.

Study used PET (positron emission tomography), not fMRI; thus it fails the fMRI requirement. Although it involves task-based script judgment in healthy adults, the modality is non-fMRI, so exclusion criterion E4 applies.

The study focuses on resting-state fMRI and methods for recovering effective connectivity; no task-based paradigm or problem-solving/reasoning task is described. It is a resting-state/connectivity-only study, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

This is an fMRI study of biological motion perception in OCD patients, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. The abstract does not indicate inclusion of a healthy adult control group. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study of healthy participants examining spontaneous low-frequency BOLD fluctuations; it does not involve any task-based problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only).

Resting-state simultaneous fMRI/LFP study conducted in rats (non-human). Not a task-based fMRI study of humans performing problem-solving; therefore fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria for resting-state and non-human sample.
Study involves a single 3-month-old infant with perinatal stroke, not healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult sample.
Study uses task-based fMRI during sexual activity, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not address the review objective (problem solving in healthy adults), so it is excluded.
Animal (rat) study using BOLD fMRI and pharmacological manipulation with forepaw stimulation; no human participants or healthy adult (18-65) sample, and not a problem-solving task in adults.

The abstract describes resting-state brain activity in bvFTD patients (brain activity at rest), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, and does not report task-related activation. Therefore it meets the resting-state exclusion criterion.

This is a task-based fMRI study with human participants performing arithmetic problem-solving tasks (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and reports task-related BOLD activations. Participants are described as nonclinical; satisfies inclusion criteria.

This study reports simultaneous EEG-fMRI methods and visual evoked potential/eyes-open paradigms, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults; therefore it does not meet the review’s focus on problem-solving tasks.

The article is a review of fMRI methodologies and applications in nutrition, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is therefore a review article and meets the review/meta-analysis exclusion criterion.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI with task-related activations in healthy subjects, but participants are described as "elderly" (age range not specified) and it is unclear if the task meets the review's problem-solving definition; full-text needed to confirm age eligibility and task classification.

Abstract describes a resting-state fMRI study of resting state networks using ultra-fast imaging; no task or problem-solving paradigm or task-related activations are reported. Excluded as a resting-state/connectivity study (E2).

The study examines tongue motor activations and compares BOLD with optical signals; it is not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI and reports activations (I1, I4), it does not meet the key inclusion criterion of a problem-solving task (I2).

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study (no task-based paradigm) focused on declarative memory networks; participants did not perform problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

The fMRI task is a monetary incentive delay (reward anticipation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving), so it is not relevant to the review objective.

Study examined five patients with persistent left neglect after right stroke—no healthy adult participants. It does not address task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults and thus fails the healthy-sample criterion.

This fMRI study used passive observation of social interactions (movie clips), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it meets I1 (task-based fMRI), I3 (healthy adult sample), and I4 (reports task-related activation), it does not satisfy I2 (participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task). Exclusion is based on failure to meet the required task domain for the review.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of verbal working memory with pharmacological manipulation, but it does not clearly state that participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task nor whether the sample comprises healthy adults. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3 — include for full-text review.

Study uses a visual oddball attention task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). It is task-based fMRI and reports activation, but does not address the review objective of problem-solving tasks.
Study uses an emotion-recognition task with infant faces, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). It is task-based fMRI in adults and reports activation.
Participants are adolescent girls (not adults 18–65), so the study does not include a healthy adult sample required for the review. The abstract does not indicate adult participants.
The study examines saccade and vergence eye-movement networks using fMRI and ICA, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

The study models resting-state functional networks and sleep-related connectivity changes, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It focuses on resting-state/connectivity and modeling rather than a task-based activation paradigm.

This is a methods-focused, connectivity (Granger causality) fMRI study that emphasizes ROI connectivity analysis. Although an emotion-face task was used, the paper targets connectivity detection rather than task-based problem-solving activation. It therefore qualifies as a connectivity-only study and does not address problem solving in healthy adults (E2).

Methods paper on arterial spin labeling (ASL) CBF mapping comparing ASL and PET; not a task-based BOLD fMRI study of problem solving. No problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults is reported. This is a non-fMRI/methods imaging study, so exclusion criterion E4 applies.
Study is in macaque (non-human) subjects, not healthy adult humans; also does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review population or task criteria.

The study investigates emotional processing of music and faces (audiovisual emotion congruence), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 for the review objective.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of spontaneous BOLD events and connectivity, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study. It does not involve participants performing a problem-solving task, so it meets the resting-state exclusion.

The study is a resting-state combined EEG-fMRI investigation of resting state networks, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving or reasoning. It does not involve participants performing a problem-solving task, so it meets the resting-state exclusion.

Task-based fMRI study of arithmetic problem solving reporting task-related activations (angular gyrus, ACC/SMA). Abstract confirms human participants performing problem-solving. It does not explicitly state participant ages or health status, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

The study used PET imaging rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the requirement for task-based fMRI studies (E4). Additionally, participants are older adults (aging study) likely beyond the 18–65 range. Exclusion is clear based on imaging modality.
The study used functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIR), an optical imaging modality, not task-based fMRI. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).
This is a methods fMRI paper using finger-tapping and visual stimulation tasks, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. It does not address the review objective (I2), so it should be excluded.

The study is a resting-state fMRI investigation of ALFF reliability and does not involve any task-based problem-solving or report task-related activations. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state study).

Abstract describes a historical first-person account of the discovery of BOLD fMRI rather than a task-based fMRI experiment. It does not report data from human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, nor task-related activation results.

Study uses cue-reactivity fMRI in cocaine abusers and PET in healthy subjects; it does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

Study reports task-based simultaneous EEG/fMRI with task-related BOLD responses and includes healthy adult controls. However, the task is described as voluntary selection/decision-making rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task, so abstract is insufficient to confirm I2. Recommend inclusion for full-text review to determine if the task meets the problem-solving criterion.

Abstract reports task-related brain activations during decision-making/reward processing, consistent with a task-based neuroimaging study; however it does not specify that the task involves problem-solving/reasoning or that participants are healthy adults. Include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

The study uses an emotion/picture-viewing task probing autonomic responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2). It is task-based fMRI and reports activations, but does not address the review objective of problem solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based BOLD fMRI study reporting activation dynamics (primary visual cortex) but does not involve or mention problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Insufficient information to confirm presence of a problem-solving task—include for full-text review.

Methods/simulation paper generating synthetic EEG/fMRI data to benchmark connectivity metrics; no human participants or task-based problem-solving tasks reported. Focus is on connectivity/simulations rather than task-based fMRI activation in healthy adults.

Study investigates haemodynamic changes related to epileptic seizures using EEG-fMRI and intracranial EEG in patients. It does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective or include a healthy adult sample.

This is a review article summarizing SPECT, PET, and MRS findings after mild TBI, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and focuses on non-fMRI modalities (E4).

This is a technical review article of the VASO MRI method, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in humans. It does not report task-related brain activation in healthy adults and meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation reporting task-related BOLD activations for discrimination and categorization tasks in human participants. However, the abstract does not make clear whether these tasks meet the review's requirement of problem-solving or reasoning, nor does it state participant ages. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is an fMRI study of object perception using real-time stimulus selection, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (task must involve problem solving/reasoning) and is not relevant to the review objective.

The study examines neural responses to smoking and monetary rewards in smokers during nicotine deprivation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, and does not report a healthy adult (non-smoking) sample. Fails review scope (no healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes a methodological comparison of BOLD signal characteristics for SSFP sequences at 7T with no mention of problem-solving tasks, participant details, or task-related activations. Insufficient information to determine whether it involves task-based problem solving in healthy adults; include for full-text review.
This is not an fMRI study: it uses SPECT and PET imaging and does not report task-based fMRI results or problem-solving tasks in healthy adults, so it fails the primary modality criterion.

The study reports resting-state EEG-fMRI in patients with idiopathic generalized epilepsies, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm, and includes clinical patients only (no healthy adult sample). Does not meet inclusion criteria.

This study reports resting-state BOLD functional connectivity in the spinal cord with no task or problem-solving paradigm. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it meets the resting-state/connectivity exclusion.

Abstract focuses on methods for inferring effective connectivity (connectivity-only analyses and simulations) rather than task-based problem-solving activation in healthy adults; does not report task-related activation results. Excluded per E2.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activation (finger-to-thumb tapping), the task is motor, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning) and is not relevant to the review objective.

Study uses task-based fMRI of pupil-LC relationships during an oddball task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet I2 (problem-solving task) required for the review. It does report task-related activation and uses fMRI (I1, I4).

The study uses task-based fMRI and reports task-related BOLD activation linked to EEG and behavior. However, the task is auditory categorization (not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task) and participant ages/health status are not specified in the abstract. Insufficient information to determine whether it meets the review’s problem-solving and healthy adult criteria, so include for full-text review.

Participants are schizophrenia patients on antipsychotics with no healthy adult sample; thus does not meet requirement for healthy adult participants. While it is a task-based fMRI study reporting activation, it lacks healthy adults and focuses on a clinical population (E3).
Study is an animal (rat) fMRI experiment using forepaw electrical stimulation, not a human problem-solving task; does not include healthy adult humans.

This is a task-based fMRI/MEG/EEG study of visual short-term memory maintenance (VSTM), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails the review’s key inclusion criterion I2, despite reporting task-related activations in healthy adults.

The study used PET imaging rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the requirement for task-based fMRI studies (E4). Although it involves healthy adult participants performing a task and reports activation, the imaging modality excludes it from this review.

Study uses fMRI with a hypercapnia (breathing) stimulus to assess vasoreactivity in Parkinson's patients; it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults, so it fails the key inclusion criterion I2 and is not relevant to the review objective.

The study uses a semantic verbal fluency/word-generation task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult controls and reports activation, but the task is outside the review’s scope.

The study tests Parkinson’s disease patients (no healthy adult sample) and uses gait/freezing motor tasks rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks; thus it fails the population and task relevance criteria.

Task-based fMRI of somatosensory stimulation in comatose cardiac-arrest patients and controls; does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults, thus fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving).

Abstract describes MRI methodology and mentions task activation but does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task, participant ages/health, or report task-related activation results for problem solving. Insufficient information to determine eligibility, so include for full-text review.

The study used a choice reaction time sensorimotor task (visual and response-choice demands), not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2). It is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations, but does not meet the review's core task criterion.

This is a methodological fMRI study using transient hypercapnia as a physiological stimulus, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning study in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activation for problem solving, so it does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria (particularly I2).

The study investigates brain responses to phobia-related pictures (emotion/fear processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. While it is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

This study is a resting-state/connectivity investigation of motion effects on R-fMRI metrics, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.

Study tests an ASL PVE correction method in patients with asymptomatic carotid occlusive disease during rest and motor activation. It does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task and the sample is clinical patients, not healthy adults (18–65).

The study examines intrinsic/resting-state BOLD oscillations after ingestion, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It therefore meets exclusion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-focused).

Abstract reports an event-related task-based fMRI study with task-related BOLD activation. It is unclear from the abstract whether the task qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task and participant ages/health status are not specified; include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

The study uses an n-back spatial working memory task in patients with hepatic encephalopathy — this is not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). It is a task-based fMRI study with healthy controls and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 satisfied) but does not address the review objective.
This is a resting-state fMRI study comparing eyes-closed vs eyes-open (no task or problem-solving paradigm). It does not meet the task-based problem-solving requirement, thus excluded under E2.

This fMRI study examines blink-related visual cortex responses during visual fixation tasks, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks (I2 not met). Although it is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activation, it does not address the review objective of problem solving.

Study investigates acute heroin effects on negative emotional processing in drug addiction, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. Likely sample are individuals with heroin dependence (no clear healthy adult group). Not relevant to the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy middle-aged adults performing n-back working memory tasks; reports fMRI task effects. However, n-back is a working-memory task rather than an explicit problem-solving/reasoning task, so inclusion for the problem-solving review is uncertain and requires full-text review to confirm task relevance.

The abstract describes biophysical modeling of BOLD signals and parameter sensitivity analyses; it does not report any human participants performing a problem-solving task, nor task-related fMRI activation results. Therefore it lacks a healthy adult sample and task-based activation data.

This study uses ictal-fMRI in patients with pharmaco-resistant focal epilepsy to localize seizure onset. It does not involve healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it falls outside the review scope.

This is an animal (rat) fMRI study of somatosensory activation using electrical forepaw stimulation; it does not include healthy adult human participants or a problem-solving task, so it fails the review’s population/task criteria.

This study uses PET (fluorodopa and H2[15]O) to measure resting regional cerebral blood flow and dopamine synthesis in Parkinson/Gaucher patients and carriers. It is not a task-based fMRI study and involves resting-state imaging, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study reporting ventral striatal BOLD prediction-error correlates with fluid IQ. It is unclear whether the probabilistic reversal learning task meets the review's definition of a problem-solving/reasoning task and the sample health/age is not specified, so include for full-text review.

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation in patients with idiopathic generalized epilepsy, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults. It lacks healthy adult participants and does not report task-related activation.

fMRI task with simultaneous eye-tracking reports BOLD differences for false- vs true-belief trials, but abstract does not clearly state participants' ages or explicitly label the task as problem-solving/reasoning. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3; include for full-text review.

Participants are Parkinson’s disease patients with depression (no healthy adult sample) and the task involved emotional stimuli, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the review’s population or task requirements.

The study investigates resting-state deactivation and effective connectivity (pgACC–sgACC) using fMRI-MRS during a simple task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; its primary focus is resting-state/connectivity mechanisms, meeting exclusion E2.

Abstract describes methodological review of fMRI analysis approaches (MVPA, encoding models) rather than reporting an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, so excluded under E1.

This is a perceptual decision-making (face detection) fMRI study, not a problem-solving or reasoning task—does not meet inclusion criterion I2. The abstract indicates task-based fMRI with reported activations (meets I1 and I4), but the task falls outside the review’s problem-solving scope.

Abstract focuses on cognitive impairment in schizophrenia and social functioning; it does not mention task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or inclusion of healthy adult participants. Does not meet requirement for healthy adult fMRI problem-solving studies.

This is a computational/modeling study simulating EEG and fMRI BOLD signals using mean field models; it does not report task-based fMRI data or involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks, nor report task-related activations.

Abstract describes a methodological fMRI analysis applied to experimental paradigms and reports activation maps, but it does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task or participant characteristics. Insufficient information about task type and sample to determine exclusion, so include for full-text review.

This is a PET (H2^15O-PET) activation study rather than an fMRI study, so it fails the non-fMRI exclusion criterion. Although it involves healthy adults performing a planning/problem-solving task and reports task-related activations, the imaging modality excludes it from a task-based fMRI review.

Abstract mentions functional MRI but provides insufficient information about whether the fMRI was task-based, whether participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task, or whether healthy adults were included. Include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

This is an animal (rat) fMRI study examining methylene blue effects on sensory-evoked responses and metabolism, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore lacks a healthy adult human sample and does not involve problem-solving tasks.

This is a PET (H2 15O) study of selective attention (Go/NoGo) in schizophrenic patients and controls, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore fails the modality criterion (E4) and does not address problem-solving.

Study uses memory-encoding tasks in aMCI subjects, not problem-solving tasks in healthy adults; abstract indicates only clinical (aMCI) participants, so no healthy adult sample. Does not meet review objective.

The study uses songbirds (non-human subjects) performing auditory listening tasks rather than healthy adult humans performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks; therefore it does not meet the human adult sample requirement.

This is a review article about resting-state fMRI in non-human primates, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans. It is a review (E1), focuses on resting-state/connectivity (E2), and involves non-human subjects (E3).

This is a visual sensory stimulation fMRI study (flickering checkerboard) demonstrating laminar BOLD responses in V1, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

The study uses task-based fMRI of visual perception (moving gratings) and reports EEG coherence and localized BOLD in occipital visual areas, but does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract describes a methodological/review discussion of fMR-adaptation rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI experiment of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review/methods paper, so excluded under E1.

Study investigates EMG-fMRI for tremor pathophysiology and surgical targeting in patients with essential tremor; it does not involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks or a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract describes a clinical neuroanatomical study of left anterior thalamic infarction patients focusing on cognitive/behavioural disturbances; it does not mention task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or inclusion of healthy adult participants. Therefore it fails the fMRI and healthy-sample inclusion criteria.
